blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ III
INFINITESIMAL EXTENSIONSby M. Demazure

In this exposé, we place ourselves in the following general situation. We have a scheme S and a coherent nilpotent ideal \(I\) on S. We denote by \(S_n\) the closed subscheme of S defined by the ideal \(I^{n+1}\) (\(n \geqslant 0\)). In particular \(S_0\) is defined by \(I\). As \(I\) is nilpotent, \(S_n\) is equal to S for n large enough and the \(S_i\) have the same underlying topological space. A typical example of this situation is the following: S is the spectrum of an Artinian local ring A, \(I\) is the ideal defined by the radical of A, so \(S_0\) is the spectrum of the residue field of A.

In the preceding situation, we are given a certain number of data over \(S_0\) and we look for data over S lifting them, that is, giving them back by base change from S to \(S_0\). This is done step by step, through the intermediary of the \(S_n\). At each step, we propose to define the obstructions encountered and to classify, when they exist, the solutions obtained.

The passage from \(S_n\) to \(S_{n+1}\) can be generalized as follows: we have a scheme S, two ideals \(I\) and \(J\) with \(I \supset J\), \(I \cdot J = 0\) (in the preceding case S, \(I\) and \(J\) are respectively \(S_{n+1}\), \(I/I^{n+2}\), \(I^{n+1}/I^{n+2}\)). We denote by \(S_0\) (resp. \(S_J\)) the closed subscheme of S defined by \(I\) (resp. \(J\)) and we pose an extension problem from \(S_J\) to S.

In SGA 1 III the problems of extension of morphisms of schemes and of extension of schemes were treated. We shall here pose the problems of extension of group morphisms, of extension of group structures and of extension of subgroups.

We have collected in Section 0 the results of SGA 1 III which will be useful to us, in order to put them in the form most convenient for our purpose, and to spare the reader from having to refer constantly to SGA 1 III. Section 1 collects computations of group cohomology which will be useful later on and which have nothing to do with the theory of schemes. Sections 2 and 3 treat respectively the extension of group morphisms and the extension of group structures. In Section 4, we have quickly recalled the proof of a result stated in TDTE IV concerning the extension of subschemes, and applied this result to the problem of extension of subgroups. For the sequel of the Seminar, only the result of Section 2, concerning the extension of group morphisms, will be indispensable.

The idea of reducing the infinitesimal extension problems to the usual cohomology computations in group extensions was suggested by J. GIRAUD at the time of the oral exposé (whose computations were distinctly more complicated and less transparent). Unfortunately, it seems that this method applies well only to the first two problems studied, and we have not been able to escape rather painful computations in the case of extensions of subgroups.

To simplify the language, we shall call a Y-functor, resp. Y-scheme, …, a functor, resp. scheme, …, equipped with a morphism into the functor Y, thereby extending the definitions of Exposé I (which concerned only the case of a representable Y).

pages.title: 0. Reminders from SGA 1 III and various remarks

blocks.html: 0. Reminders from SGA 1 III and various remarks
Let us first state a general definition.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 0.1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category, X an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), G a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group acting on X. We say that X is formally principal homogeneous under G if the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:

(i) for each object S of \(\mathscr{C}\), the set \(X(S)\) is empty or principal homogeneous under \(G(S)\);

(ii) the morphism of functors \(G \times X \to X \times X\) defined set-theoretically by \((g,x) \mapsto (gx,x)\) is an isomorphism.

This done, we shall put the results of SGA 1 III into the form which will be most useful to us. We shall employ the following general notation throughout this section. We have a scheme S and on S two quasi-coherent ideals \(I\) and \(J\) such that \[ I \supset J \qquad \text{and} \qquad I \cdot J = 0. \] We shall thus have in particular \(J^2 = 0\). We denote by \(S_0\) (resp. \(S_J\)) the closed subscheme of S defined by the ideal \(I\) (resp. \(J\)). For every S-functor X, we shall systematically denote by \(X_0\) and \(X_J\) the functors obtained by base change from S to \(S_0\) and \(S_J\). Same notation for a morphism.

Let X be an S-functor. Define a functor \(X^+\) over S by the formula: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y, X^+) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S_J}(Y_J, X_J) \] for a variable S-scheme Y. In the notation of Exp. II, 1, we have \[ X^+ \simeq \prod_{S_J/S} X_J. \]
The identity morphism of \(X_J\) defines by construction an S-morphism \(p_X : X \longrightarrow X^+\).

Let us now remark that if X is an S-group functor, then \(X_J\) is an \(S_J\)-group functor, and the defining formula of \(X^+\) endows it with a structure of S-group functor. We then have \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(Y, X^+) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S_J\text{-gr.}}(Y_J, X_J) \] for every S-group functor Y. The morphism \(p_X\) is therefore a morphism of S-group functors.

Let us now return to the general case, but suppose that X is an S-scheme. An S-morphism from a variable S-scheme Y to \(X^+\) being by definition an \(S_J\)-morphism \(g_J\) from \(Y_J\) to \(X_J\), we define an \(X^+\)-functor in abelian groups \(\mathrm{L}_X\), by setting for every \(X^+\)-scheme Y \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(Y, \mathrm{L}_X) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(g_0^*(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}), J\mathscr{O}_Y), \] where \(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\) denotes the Module of relative differentials of \(X_0\) with respect to \(S_0\) (SGA 1, I.1), and where we regard \(J\mathscr{O}_Y\) as an \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\)-module thanks to the fact that it is annihilated by \(I\).

The \(X^+\)-functor \(\mathrm{L}_X\) is an abelian \(X^+\)-group. Moreover it depends functorially on X: let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be an S-morphism; from it we deduce by functoriality an S-morphism \[ f^+ : X^+ \longrightarrow Y^+ \] compatible with \(f\), that is, such that the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{f} \ar[d]_-{p_X} & Y \ar[d]^-{p_Y} \\ X^+ \ar[r]_-{f^+} & Y^+ } \] Let us define an S-morphism \(\mathrm{L}_f : \mathrm{L}_X \to \mathrm{L}_Y\) compatible with \(f^+\), hence such that we have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{L}_X \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{L}_f} \ar[d] & \mathrm{L}_Y \ar[d] \\ X^+ \ar[r]_-{f^+} & Y^+ } \] we have a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_0}\)-Modules (SGA 1 II, 4) \[ f_0^*(\Omega^1_{Y_0/S_0}) \longrightarrow \Omega^1_{X_0/S_0} \] which for every \(X^+\)-scheme Z defines a morphism of abelian groups, functorial in Z \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(Z, \mathrm{L}_X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{Y^+}(Z, \mathrm{L}_Y), \] which gives us the desired morphism.

If X and \(X'\) are two S-schemes, we have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{L}_X \times_S \mathrm{L}_{X'} \ar[d] \ar[r]^-{\simeq} & \mathrm{L}_{X\times_S X'} \ar[d] \\ X^+ \times_S X'^+ \ar[r]_-{\simeq} & (X \times_S X')^+ } \]
We can now state:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 0.2

For every S-scheme X, one can define an action of the abelian \(X^+\)-group \(\mathrm{L}_X\) on the \(X^+\)-object X (on the left), such that:

(i) this action makes X a formally principal homogeneous object under \(\mathrm{L}_X\) over \(X^+\): the morphism \[ \mathrm{L}_X \underset{X^+}{\times} X \longrightarrow X \underset{X^+}{\times} X \] is an isomorphism;

(ii) this action is functorial in the S-scheme X: for every S-morphism \(f : X \to Y\), the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{L}_X \underset{X^+}{\times} X \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{L}_f\times_{f^+} f} & X \ar[d]^-{f} \\ \mathrm{L}_Y \underset{Y^+}{\times} Y \ar[r] & Y } \]

(iii) this action "commutes with fibre products": for all S-schemes X and \(X'\), the following diagram is commutative \[ \xymatrix{ (\mathrm{L}_{X'}\times_{X'^+}X')\times_S(\mathrm{L}_X\times_{X^+}X) \ar[rr] \ar[d]_-{\simeq} & & X'\times_S X \\ (\mathrm{L}_{X'}\times_S \mathrm{L}_X)\times_{(X'^+\times_S X^+)}(X'\times_S X) \ar[r]_-{\simeq} & \mathrm{L}_{X'\times_S X}\times_{(X'\times_S X)^+}(X'\times_S X) \ar[ur]_-{\simeq} & } \]

In SGA III 5.1 the following is proved: for every S-scheme Y and every \(S_J\)-morphism \(g_J : Y_J \to X_J\), the set of S-morphisms \(g : Y \longrightarrow X\) reducing to \(g_J\) is empty or principal homogeneous under the commutative group \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_J}}(h^*(\Omega^1_{X/S}), J\mathscr{O}_Y), \] where \(h\) is the composite morphism \(Y_J \xrightarrow{\ g_J\ } X_J \to X\). Translated into our language, this means exactly that for every \(X^+\)-scheme Y, the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(Y,X)\) is empty or principal homogeneous under the above group. Let us first show that this group is none other than \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(Y, \mathrm{L}_X) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(g_0^*(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}), J\mathscr{O}_Y). \]

For this it suffices to remark that \(J\cdot\mathscr{O}_Y\) is annihilated by \(I\mathscr{O}_Y\), hence that we may replace \(h^*(\Omega^1_{X/S})\) by the sheaf it induces on \(Y_0\), which one sees without difficulty to be isomorphic to \(g_0^*(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0})\).

This done, to complete the proof one would have to verify that the construction of SGA 1, III 5.1 is functorial in the \(X^+\)-scheme Y, and then that the action thus defined does enjoy the functorial properties required in (ii) and (iii). We shall not do so; the sceptical reader will consult EGA IV on this subject, where he will perhaps find some elements of a proof.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 0.3

Suppose the \(X^+\)-scheme Y flat over S (SGA IV). One can then write \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(Y, \mathrm{L}_X) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(g_0^*(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}), J \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}). \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 0.4

Suppose that \(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\) is the inverse image on \(X_0\) of an \(\mathscr{O}_{S_0}\)-Module denoted \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\) (this case will occur in particular when \(X_0\) is an \(S_0\)-group, cf. II, 4.11). If we define a functor \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) over S by the formula \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y, \mathrm{L}'_X) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}, J\mathscr{O}_Y), \] we shall have \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(Y, \mathrm{L}_X) = \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y, \mathrm{L}'_X) \] for every \(X^+\)-scheme Y, that is, \[ \mathrm{L}_X = \mathrm{L}'_X \underset{S}{\times} X^+. \] The morphism of \(X^+\)-group functors \(\mathrm{L}_X \to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{X^+}(X)\) will translate into a morphism of S-group functors \(\mathrm{L}'_X \to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(X)\), the actions of \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) on X respecting the morphism \(p_X : X \to X^+\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 0.5

Under the preceding conditions, suppose moreover that Y is an S-scheme flat over S. We then have \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(Y, \mathrm{L}_X) = \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y, \mathrm{L}'_X) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(Y_0, \mathrm{L}_{0X}), \] where the \(S_0\)-functor in abelian groups \(\mathrm{L}_{0X}\) is defined by the following identity (with respect to the variable \(S_0\)-scheme T): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(T, \mathrm{L}_{0X}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_T}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_T, J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_T). \] In the notation of II, 1, we have thus shown that the functors \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) and \(\prod_{S_0/S} \mathrm{L}_{0X}\) have the same restriction to the full subcategory of \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\) whose objects are the S-schemes Y flat over S.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 0.6

Suppose now that \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\) admits a finite presentation (EGA 0, 5.2.5), which will in particular be the case if \(X_0\) is locally of finite presentation over \(S_0\). One can write \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(T,\mathrm{L}_{0X}) = \Gamma(T, \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_T}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\otimes\mathscr{O}_T, J\otimes\mathscr{O}_T)) \] (the tensor products being taken over \(\mathscr{O}_{S_0}\)). If T is flat over \(S_0\), it follows from EGA \(0_{\mathrm I}\), 6.7.6 that this can also be written \[ \Gamma(T, \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J)\otimes\mathscr{O}_T). \] Introducing the notation \(\mathbf{W}(\ )\) of I, 4.6.1, we have thus proved that for every \(S_0\)-scheme T flat over \(S_0\), we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(T,\mathrm{L}_{0X}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(T, \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J))). \] In summary, if \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\) admits a finite presentation, and if we restrict to the category of S-schemes flat over S, we have \[ \mathrm{L}'_X = \prod_{S_0/S} \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J)) \] (the sheaf of which we take the \(\mathbf{W}\) being quasi-coherent by EGA I, 9.1.1). Let us remark finally that if \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\) is moreover locally free (of finite rank), for example if \(X_0\) is smooth over \(S_0\) (in which case it is automatically locally of finite presentation over \(S_0\)), we have \[ \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J) \simeq \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J, \] where, by abuse of language (\(X_0\) not necessarily being an \(S_0\)-group), we denote by \(\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\) the dual of the \(\mathscr{O}_{S_0}\)-Module \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\).
The preceding proposition has an important corollary:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 0.7

Let X be an S-scheme. Every S-endomorphism of X inducing the identity on \(X_J\) is an automorphism. We have an exact sequence of groups \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J\mathscr{O}_X) \xrightarrow{\ i\ } \mathrm{Aut}_S(X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Aut}_{S_J}(X_J). \] Moreover, if we let \(\mathrm{Aut}_S(X)\) act on the first group by transport of structure, we have the following relation \[ i(um) = u\,i(m)\,u^{-1} \] for all \(u \in \mathrm{Aut}_S(X)\) and \(m \in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J\mathscr{O}_X)\).

By (i), \(\mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(X,X)\) is a principal homogeneous set under \(\mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(X,\mathrm{L}_X)\), for it is certainly non-empty; indeed it contains a marked point: the identity automorphism of X. Thanks to this marked point, one defines an isomorphism of sets \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(X, \mathrm{L}_X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(X, X). \] Applying now (ii) to an endomorphism of X over \(X^+\), that is, an S-endomorphism of X inducing the identity on \(X_J\), one sees easily that the preceding map respects the composition laws of the two sides. It follows first that every element of \(\mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(X,X)\) is invertible, which is the first assertion of the statement, and then that we have an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(X, \mathrm{L}_X) \xrightarrow{\ i\ } \mathrm{Aut}_S(X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Aut}_{S_J}(X_J), \] which is the second.

Let us now remark that the morphism \(i\) defined above is functorial in X for isomorphisms, for it is defined in structural terms from the action of \(\mathrm{L}_X\) on X over \(X^+\), itself functorial in X by assertion (ii) of Proposition 0.2. So let \(u\) be an automorphism of X over S. It defines a map \[ h : \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(X,\mathrm{L}_X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(X,\mathrm{L}_X) \] by transport of structure and a map \[ f : \mathrm{Aut}_S(X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Aut}_S(X) \] likewise by transport of structure, that is, by the commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{a} \ar[d]_-{u} & X \ar[d]^-{u} \\ X \ar[r]_-{f(a)} & X } \] Writing \(i(h(m)) = f(i(m))\), one finds the desired formula.

Now let X be an S-scheme such that \(X_J\) is an \(S_J\)-group. Suppose there exists an S-morphism \[ \mathrm{P} : X \underset{S}{\times} X \longrightarrow X \] such that the morphism obtained by base change \[ \mathrm{P}_J : X_J \underset{S_J}{\times} X_J \longrightarrow X_J \] is the group law of \(X_J\). (An important special case of the preceding situation will be the case where X is an S-group and where we take for P its group law). From it we deduce a morphism \[ \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{P}} : \mathrm{L}_X \underset{S}{\times} \mathrm{L}_X \longrightarrow \mathrm{L}_X \] which, in fact, does not depend on P, for it can be computed by means of the group law \(\mathrm{P}_J\) of \(X_J\), as we shall now see. The morphism \[ \mathrm{P}_0^*(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}) \longrightarrow \Omega^1_{X_0\times_{S_0}X_0/S_0} \] translates in \(\mathrm{T}_{X_0/S_0}\) (II 4.1) into the group law of the latter, which one writes down immediately with the help of the decomposition of \(\mathrm{T}_{X_0/S_0}\) as a semi-direct product of \(\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\) by \(X_0\). Using (ii) and (iii) of 0.2, one obtains, after all the computations:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 0.8

Let \(\mathrm{P} : X\times_S X \to X\) be an S-morphism such that \(\mathrm{P}_J\) endows \(X_J\) with a structure of \(S_J\)-group. Denote by \((m,x)\mapsto mx\) the morphism \[ \mathrm{L}'_X \underset{S}{\times} X \longrightarrow X \] defining the action of \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) (defined in 0.4) on X. Denote by \(\mathrm{Ad} : X^+ \longrightarrow \mathrm{Aut}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{L}'_X)\) the action of \(X^+\) on \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) deduced from the adjoint action of \(X_0\) on \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\). For every \(S' \to S\) and all \(x,x' \in X(S')\), \(m,m' \in \mathrm{L}'_X(S')\), we have: \[ \mathrm{P}(mx, m'x') = (m\cdot \mathrm{Ad}\,p_X(x)(m'))\,\mathrm{P}(x,x'). \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 0.9

Let X be an S-group. Then \(X^+\) is naturally equipped with a structure of S-group, \(p_X\) is a morphism of S-groups and the S-morphism \(i : \mathrm{L}'_X \longrightarrow X\) defined set-theoretically by \(m \mapsto me\) (where \(e\) is the unit section of G) is an isomorphism of S-groups from \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) onto the kernel of \(p_X : X \to X^+\).

The first two assertions have already been proved. As X is formally principal homogeneous over \(X^+\) under \(\mathrm{L}_X = \mathrm{L}'_X \times_S X^+\), the stated morphism is indeed an isomorphism (of S-functors) from \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) onto the kernel of \(p_X\). The fact that it respects the group structures follows from the explicit formula of 0.8.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 0.10

With the preceding notation, for every \(S' \to S\) and all \(x \in X(S')\) and \(m \in \mathrm{L}'_X(S')\), we have \[ x\,i(m)\,x^{-1} = i(\mathrm{Ad}\,p_X(x)\,m). \] This follows from the formula given above and from \(i(m)x = mx\).

When X is an S-group, we have thus determined explicitly the kernel of \(X \to X^+\) and the action of the inner automorphisms of X on this kernel. We shall now see that one can do the same for certain S-group functors which are not necessarily representable. One case will be useful to us, that of the functors \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\) (I 1.7). Let us state right away:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 0.11

Let E be an S-scheme. Set \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(E)\). The kernel of the morphism of S-group functors \(p_X : X \longrightarrow X^+\) is canonically identified with the S-functor in commutative groups \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) defined by \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y, \mathrm{L}'_X) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{E_0\times_{S_0}Y_0}}(\Omega^1_{E_0/S_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}, J\mathscr{O}_{E\times_S Y}) \] where Y denotes a variable S-scheme.

Indeed, if Y is a variable S-scheme, we have: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X) = \mathrm{Aut}_Y(E\times_S Y), \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X^+) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S_J}(Y_J,X_J) = \mathrm{Aut}_{Y_J}(E\underset{S}{\times}Y_J) = \mathrm{Aut}_{Y_J}((E\underset{S}{\times}Y)\underset{Y}{\times}Y_J). \] Applying 0.7 to the Y-scheme \(E\times_S Y\), we obtain an isomorphism of S-groups \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,\mathrm{L}'_X) \simeq \mathrm{Ker}\bigl(\mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X)\longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X^+)\bigr), \] an isomorphism which one easily verifies to be functorial in the S-scheme Y, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 0.12

There is a natural action \(f\) of X on \(\mathrm{L}'_X\), defined in the following manner: for every \(Y \to S\), we have \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X) = \mathrm{Aut}_Y(\mathrm{F})\) and \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,\mathrm{L}'_X) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{F_0}}(\Omega^1_{F_0/Y_0}, J\mathscr{O}_F), \] where F is the Y-scheme \(E\times_S Y\); the first group acts on the second by transport of structure and this action is indeed functorial in Y. We then have the formula \[ x\,i(m)\,x^{-1} = i(f(x)m), \] for every \(Y \to S\) and all \(x \in \mathrm{Hom}_S(S', X)\), \(m \in \mathrm{Hom}_S(S', \mathrm{L}'_X)\); it suffices indeed to apply 0.7 to the Y-scheme F.

blocks.title: Reminder
blocks.html:
Reminder 0.13

The direct image of a quasi-coherent module under a morphism of finite presentation is quasi-coherent. Under the same conditions, the formation of the direct image commutes with flat base change: in the situation \[ \xymatrix{ T \ar[d]_-{f} & T' = T\times_S S' \ar[l]_-{g'} \ar[d]^-{f'} \\ S & S' \ar[l]^-{g} } \] if we suppose \(f\) (and hence \(f'\)) of finite presentation and \(g\) (and hence \(g'\)) flat, we have for every quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_T\)-module \(\mathscr{F}\) \[ f_*(\mathscr{F})\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_{S'} = f'_*(\mathscr{F}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_{S'}), \] or, more aesthetically, \[ g^*(f_*(\mathscr{F})) = f'_*(g'^*(\mathscr{F})). \] These two facts are more generally valid for a quasi-compact and quasi-separated morphism (cf. EGA III.1.4.15 in the quasi-compact separated case and EGA III 6.9.10).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 0.14

In the notation of 0.11, suppose E of finite presentation over S and Y flat over S. We then have successively \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,\mathrm{L}'_X) = \Gamma(E\times_S Y, \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_E}(\Omega^1_{E/S}, J\mathscr{O}_E)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_Y) \] \[ = \Gamma(Y, g_*(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_E}(\Omega^1_{E/S}, J\mathscr{O}_E)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_Y)) \] \[ = \Gamma(Y, f_*(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_E}(\Omega^1_{E/S}, J\mathscr{O}_E))\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_Y) \] \[ = \mathbf{W}(f_*(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_E}(\Omega^1_{E/S}, J\mathscr{O}_E)))(Y) \] where \(f\) and \(g\) are the structural morphisms \[ g : E\times_S Y \to Y \ , \qquad f : E \longrightarrow S. \] We have thus shown that we have \[ \mathrm{L}'_X = \mathbf{W}(f_*(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_E}(\Omega^1_{E/S}, J\mathscr{O}_E))) \] on the category of S-schemes flat over S. Note moreover that the Module of which we take the \(\mathbf{W}\) is quasi-coherent.
Let us finally extract from SGA 1 III the following two propositions.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 0.15

(SGA 1 III, 6.8) For every \(S_J\)-scheme smooth over \(S_J\) and affine, there exists an S-scheme X smooth over S reducing to \(X_J\), and such an X is unique up to (non-unique) isomorphism.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 0.16

(SGA 1 III, 5.5) Let X be an S-scheme smooth over S. For every affine S-scheme Y, the canonical map \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,p_X) : \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X^+) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S_J}(Y_J,X_J) \] is surjective.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 0.17

Let E be an S-scheme affine over S and smooth over S; set \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(E)\). For every affine S-scheme Y, the canonical map \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,p_X) : \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X^+) \] is surjective.

Indeed, \(Y\times_S E\) is affine over Y, which is itself affine, hence affine. Applying 0.16, we deduce that every \(S_J\)-morphism \(Y_J\times_{S_J}E_J \to E_J\) extends to an S-morphism \(Y\times_S E \to E\). In other words, every \(S_J\)-morphism \(Y_J \to \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{S_J}(E)\) extends to an S-morphism \(Y \to \underline{\mathrm{End}}_S(E)\). But 0.7 shows that the same holds when \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_S(E)\) is replaced by \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(E)\), which is the announced property.
1. Extensions and cohomology

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category. Let S be an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), G an S-group (representable) and F an S-functor in commutative groups on which G acts. We defined in I, 5.1 the cohomology groups \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,F)\). Recall that these are the homology groups of a complex denoted \(\mathrm{C}^*(G,F)\) where \[ \mathrm{C}^n(G,F) = \mathrm{Hom}_S((G/S)^n, F). \] As G is representable, we also have \[ \mathrm{C}^n(G,F) = F((G/S)^n)\, ; \] from this, and from the definition of the boundary operator, one sees that the complex \(\mathrm{C}^*(G,F)\) depends only on the restriction of F to the full subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}/S\) whose objects are the cartesian powers of G over S. Consequently, we have the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category, S an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), G a representable S-group. Denote by \(\mathscr{C}(G)\) the full subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}/S\) whose objects are the cartesian powers of G over S. Let F and \(F'\) be two S-functors in commutative groups on which G acts. If F and \(F'\) have the same restriction to \(\mathscr{C}(G)\), there is a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{H}^*(G,F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{H}^*(G,F'). \]

Let us state another comparison result. Now let \(T \to S\) be a morphism of \(\mathscr{C}\). If F is a T-functor in commutative groups, then \[ F_1 = \prod_{T/S} F \qquad (\text{Exp. II, 1}) \] is an S-functor in commutative groups and we have a morphism of S-group functors \[ u : \prod_{T/S} \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{T\text{-gr.}}(F) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(F_1). \] Now let G be an S-group functor and let \(G_T \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{T\text{-gr.}}(F)\) be an action of \(G_T\) on F. By definition of the functor \(\prod\), we deduce from it a morphism \[ G \longrightarrow \prod_{T/S} \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{T\text{-gr.}}(F), \] whence, by composition with \(u\), an action of G on \(F_1\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.2

Under the preceding conditions, there is a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{H}^*\Bigl(G, \prod_{T/S} F\Bigr) \simeq \mathrm{H}^*(G_T, F). \]

Indeed, by the definition of cohomology, the standard complexes are canonically isomorphic.
Following the general principles, we make the following definition:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2.1

Let \(1 \to M \xrightarrow{\ u\ } E \xrightarrow{\ v\ } G\) be a sequence of morphisms of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups. We say that it is exact if the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:

(i) for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), the sequence of ordinary groups \[ 1 \longrightarrow M(S) \xrightarrow{\ u(S)\ } E(S) \xrightarrow{\ v(S)\ } G(S) \] is exact;

(ii) for every object H of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), the sequence of ordinary groups \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(H,M) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(H,E) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(H,G) \] is exact.

Taking in particular \(H = G\) in (ii), one sees that the set of sections of \(v\) (not a priori respecting the group structures) is empty or principal homogeneous under \(\mathrm{Hom}(H,M)\). Suppose it non-empty; so let \(s : G \longrightarrow E\) be a section of \(v\). Then for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and every \(x \in G(S)\), the element \(s(x)\) of \(E(S)\) defines an inner automorphism of \(E_S\) which normalizes \(M_S\) (more correctly, the image of \(M_S\) under \(u_S\)), hence an automorphism of \(M_S\); if M is commutative, one sees "set-theoretically" that this automorphism does not depend on the section chosen, but only on \(x\), and that it depends on it multiplicatively. In summary, to every exact sequence \[ \text{(E)} \qquad 1 \longrightarrow M \xrightarrow{\ u\ } E \xrightarrow{\ v\ } G \] such that M is commutative and \(v\) has a section, is associated a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \(G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-gr.}}(M)\), which is called the action of G on M defined by the extension (E). We saw in I, 2.3.7 that \(v\) has a section which is a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups if and only if the extension (E) is isomorphic ("as an extension") to the semi-direct product of M by G relative to the preceding action. Such a section of \(v\) will be called a section of the extension (E). If \(s\) is a section of (E) and if \(m \in \Gamma(M) \simeq \mathrm{Ker}(\Gamma(E) \to \Gamma(G))\) (for the definition of \(\Gamma\), see I, 1.2), then the morphism \(G \to E\) defined by \(x \mapsto u(m)\,x\,u(m)^{-1}\) is also a section of (E), said to be deduced from \(s\) by the inner automorphism defined by \(m\) (or by \(u(m)\)).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.2

Let (E): \(1 \to M \xrightarrow{\ u\ } E \xrightarrow{\ v\ } G\) be an exact sequence of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups such that M is commutative and \(v\) has a section. Let G act on M in the manner defined by (E).

(i) The extension (E) canonically defines a class \(c(E) \in \mathrm{H}^2(G,M)\) whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of a section of (E).

(ii) If \(c(E) = 0\), the set of sections of (E) is principal homogeneous under the group \(\mathrm{Z}^1(G,M)\); the set of sections of (E) modulo the action of the inner automorphisms defined by the elements of \(\Gamma(M)\) is principal homogeneous under the group \(\mathrm{H}^1(G,M)\).

The proof proceeds exactly as in the case of ordinary groups, the fact that one starts from a section of \(v\) ensuring the functoriality of the set-theoretic computations. Let us briefly indicate the main steps of the proof.

a) To every section \(s\) of \(v\) we associate the morphism \(\mathrm{D}s : G\times G \longrightarrow M\), defined set-theoretically by \[ u(\mathrm{D}s(x,y)) = s(xy)s(y)^{-1}s(x)^{-1}. \] One shows that \(\mathrm{D}s\) is a 2-cocycle by computing \[ \partial\mathrm{D}s(x,y,z) = s(x)\mathrm{D}s(y,z)^{-1}s(x)^{-1}\mathrm{D}s(x,yz)^{-1}\mathrm{D}s(xy,z)\mathrm{D}s(x,y)\, ; \] it suffices to substitute the definition of \(\mathrm{D}s\) into this formula to find (without using any commutativity) \(\mathrm{D}s \in \mathrm{Z}^2(G,M)\).

b) If \(s\) and \(s'\) are two sections of \(v\), we have seen that there exists \(m : G \longrightarrow M\) such that \(s(x) = m(x)s'(x)\). We then have \[ \mathrm{D}s'(x,y) = m^{-1}(xy)\mathrm{D}s(x,y)s(x)m(y)s(x)^{-1}m(x), \] that is, \[ \mathrm{D}s' = \mathrm{D}s \cdot \partial m. \]

c) If \(s\) and \(s'\) are two sections of \(v\) deduced from one another by an inner automorphism of E defined by an element \(m \in \Gamma(M)\), then \(s(x) = m^{-1}s'(x)m\) implies \[ s(x) = m^{-1}s'(x)m\,s'(x)^{-1}s'(x), \] that is, \(s = \partial m \cdot s'\).

d) The reasoning is now the usual one: to find an \(s\) which is a group morphism, one must find an \(s\) such that \(\mathrm{D}s = e\), …

Let again \[ \text{(E)} \qquad 1 \longrightarrow M \xrightarrow{\ u\ } E \xrightarrow{\ v\ } G \] be an exact sequence of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups with M commutative. Let \(f : H \longrightarrow G\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups. Consider \(E_f = H\times_G E\); it is a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group and the projection \(v_f : E_f \to H\) is a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups. Likewise for \(e_f : E_f \to E\). On the other hand, if we send M into E by \(u\) and into H by the unit morphism, we define a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \(u_f : M \to E_f\). We have thus constructed a commutative diagram of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups: \[ \xymatrix{ (\mathrm{E}) & 1 \ar[r] & M \ar[r]^-{u} & E \ar[r]^-{v} & G \\ (\mathrm{E}_f) & 1 \ar[r] & M \ar[r]_-{u_f} \ar[u]^-{\mathrm{id}} & E_f \ar[r]_-{v_f} \ar[u]_-{e_f} & H \ar[u]_-{f} } \] We have immediately:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.3

(i) The sequence \((\mathrm{E}_f)\) is exact.

(ii) The map \(s \mapsto e_f\circ s = f'\) establishes a one-to-one correspondence between the morphisms \(s : H \longrightarrow E_f\) such that \(v_f\circ s = \mathrm{id}\) (that is, the sections of \(v_f\)) and the morphisms \(f' : H \longrightarrow E\) such that \(v\circ f' = f\) (that is, the morphisms \(f'\) "lifting" \(f\)).

(iii) In the preceding correspondence, sections of \((\mathrm{E}_f)\) and group morphisms \(f'\) lifting \(f\) correspond to one another.
Applying Lemma 1.2.2 to the extension \((\mathrm{E}_f)\) and taking account of 1.2.3, we obtain the following proposition (which formally contains 1.2.2):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2.4

Let (E): \(1 \to M \to E \xrightarrow{\ v\ } G\) be an exact sequence of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups with M commutative. Let \[ f : H \longrightarrow G \] be a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups; suppose that it lifts to a morphism (not necessarily of groups) \(f' : H \to E\). Let H act on M by the composite morphism (multiplicative and independent of the choice of \(f'\)), \[ H \xrightarrow{\ f'\ } E \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{int}\ } \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-gr.}}(M). \]

(i) The morphism \(f\) canonically defines a class \(c(f) \in \mathrm{H}^2(H,M)\) whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \(f' : H \longrightarrow E\) lifting \(f\).

(ii) If \(c(f) = 0\), the set of morphisms of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \(f'\) lifting \(f\), modulo the action of the inner automorphisms defined by the elements of \(\Gamma(M)\) (i.e. by the elements \(m\) of \(\Gamma(E)\) such that \(v(m)=e\)), is principal homogeneous under \(\mathrm{H}^1(H,M)\).

(iii) If \(f' : H \to E\) is a group morphism lifting \(f\), the set of transforms of \(f'\) by the inner automorphisms defined by the elements of \(\Gamma(M)\) is isomorphic to \(\Gamma(M)/\Gamma(M^H) = \Gamma(M)/\mathrm{H}^0(H,M)\).
1.3. Extensions of group laws.

Consider the following situation: we have a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) \[ p : X \longrightarrow Y \] and a commutative \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group M acting on X, such that X is formally principal homogeneous over Y under \(M_Y\). If \(g : Y \to Z\) is an arbitrary morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), then \(g\circ p : X \to Z\) is invariant under M: for each \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\), \(g\circ p(S)\) is invariant under the action of \(M(S)\) acting on \(X(S)\). We shall assume that the following condition is satisfied for \(n=1,2,3,4\).

\((+)_n\): Every morphism from \(X^n\) to M, invariant under the action of \(M^n\) acting on \(X^n\), factors in a unique way through \(p^n : X^n \to Y^n\) (where the powers \(n\) denote cartesian powers).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3.1

If \(h\) is a morphism from Y to M, the automorphism of X defined set-theoretically by \(x \mapsto h(p(x))x\) commutes with \(p\) and with the actions of M on X. This construction establishes a bijective correspondence between morphisms from Y to M and automorphisms of X commuting with \(p\) and with the actions of M.

The first part is clear. Conversely, an automorphism \(u\) of X commuting with \(p\) can be written set-theoretically as \(x \mapsto g(x)x\), where \(g\) is a certain morphism from X to M. If \(u\) commutes with the actions of M, this morphism is invariant under M and one concludes by condition \((+)_1\).

We now suppose given, in addition, a group law on Y and an action of Y on M, that is, a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups: \[ f : Y \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-gr.}}(M). \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.3.2

A composition law on X \[ \mathrm{P} : X\times X \longrightarrow X \] is called admissible if it satisfies the following two conditions:

(i) P lifts the group law of Y, i.e. the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X\times X \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{P}} \ar[d]_-{(p,p)} & X \ar[d]^-{p} \\ Y\times Y \ar[r] & Y } \] is commutative.

(ii) For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\) and all \(x,y \in X(S)\), \(m,n \in M(S)\), we have the following relation \[ (++) \qquad \mathrm{P}(mx,ny) = (m\cdot f(p(x))n)\,\mathrm{P}(x,y). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3.3

For a group law on X to be admissible, it is necessary and sufficient that the following conditions be satisfied:

(i) \(p : X \to Y\) is a group morphism.

(ii) The morphism \(i : M \to X\) defined by \(i(m) = me_X\) is an isomorphism of groups from M onto \(\mathrm{Ker}(p)\), that is: we have set-theoretically \(me_X\cdot ne_X = mne_X\).

(iii) We have \(mx = (me_X)\cdot x\) for each \(m \in M(S)\), \(x \in X(S)\).

(iv) The inner automorphisms of X act on \(\mathrm{Ker}(p)\) according to the set-theoretic formula: \[ x\,i(m)\,x^{-1} = i(f(p(x))m). \]

The proof is immediate.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3.4

Let \(u\) be an automorphism of X of the type described in 1.3.1. If P and \(\mathrm{P}'\) are two composition laws on X deduced from one another via \(u\) (by \(\mathrm{P}(ux,uy) = u\mathrm{P}'(x,y)\)), P is an admissible composition law (resp. an admissible group law) if and only if \(\mathrm{P}'\) is one.

Trivial.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.3.5

Two admissible composition laws deduced from one another by the procedure of 1.3.4 are called equivalent.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3.6

Suppose there exists an admissible composition law on X. Then:

(i) There exists a class \(c \in \mathrm{H}^3(Y,M)\) (canonically determined), whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of an associative admissible composition law on X.

(ii) If \(c=0\), the set of admissible and associative composition laws (resp. of equivalence classes of admissible and associative composition laws) on X is principal homogeneous under \(\mathrm{Z}^2(G,M)\) (resp. \(\mathrm{H}^2(G,M)\)).

The proof proceeds in several steps.

a) Let P be an admissible composition law on X. As P lifts the composition law of Y, which is associative, there exists a unique morphism \(a : X^3 \to M\) such that \[ \mathrm{P}(x,\mathrm{P}(y,z)) = a(x,y,z)\,\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{P}(x,y),z). \] Applying condition (ii), one sees at once that \(a\) is invariant under the action of \(M^3\) on \(X^3\), whence, applying hypothesis \((+)_3\), the following result:

(1) There exists a unique morphism \(\mathrm{DP} : Y^3 \to M\) such that \[ \mathrm{P}(x,\mathrm{P}(y,z)) = \mathrm{DP}(p(x),p(y),p(z))\,\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{P}(x,y),z), \] and P is associative if and only if \(\mathrm{DP} = 0\).

b) If one computes step by step \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{P}(x,y),z),t)\) using the preceding formula, one finds, setting \(p(x)=u\), \(p(y)=v\), \(p(z)=w\), \(p(t)=h\), \[ \mathrm{DP}(u,v,w)\cdot\mathrm{DP}(u,vw,h)\cdot f(u)\mathrm{DP}(v,w,h)\cdot\mathrm{DP}(u,v,wh)^{-1}\cdot\mathrm{DP}(uv,w,h)^{-1} = e_M. \] We therefore have \(\partial\mathrm{DP}(u,v,w,h) = e_M\). Since, on the other hand, the left-hand side of the preceding formula can be written, using P and \(a\), as the expression in \((x,y,z,t)\) of a certain morphism \(X^4 \to M\), it follows from the uniqueness hypothesis in \((+)_4\) that DP and \(e\), which factorize the same morphism, are equal, hence

(2) DP is a cocycle: we have \(\mathrm{DP} \in \mathrm{Z}^3(Y,M)\).

c) If P and \(\mathrm{P}'\) are two admissible composition laws on X, there exists a unique morphism \(b : X^2 \longrightarrow M\) such that \(\mathrm{P}'(x,y) = b(x,y)\mathrm{P}(x,y)\). One sees as usual that \(b\) is invariant under \(M^2\), whence:

(3) For every pair of admissible composition laws \((\mathrm{P},\mathrm{P}')\), there exists a unique \(d(\mathrm{P},\mathrm{P}') : Y^2 \to M\) with \[ \mathrm{P}'(x,y) = d(\mathrm{P},\mathrm{P}')(p(x),p(y))\,\mathrm{P}(x,y), \] and the set of admissible composition laws thus becomes principal homogeneous under \(\mathrm{Hom}(Y^2,M) = \mathrm{C}^2(G,M)\).

d) Under the preceding conditions, we have the formula:

(4) \(\mathrm{DP}' - \mathrm{DP} = \partial d(\mathrm{P},\mathrm{P}')\).

e) P and \(\mathrm{P}'\) are equivalent if and only if there exists a morphism \(u \in \mathrm{C}^1(Y,M) = \mathrm{Hom}(Y,M)\) with \(d(\mathrm{P},\mathrm{P}') = \partial u\); this follows easily from the definition of equivalence.

f) It only remains to conclude: we seek a P such that \(\mathrm{DP} = e\). Now DP is a cocycle whose class in \(\mathrm{H}^3(Y,M)\) does not depend on the admissible composition law P chosen (by (3) and (4)). This class is the required obstruction \(c\). One will be able to choose a \(\mathrm{P}'\) satisfying the conditions if and only if \(c=0\); indeed, choosing an arbitrary P, one will have to solve, by (1): \[ 0 = \mathrm{DP}' = \mathrm{DP} + \partial d(\mathrm{P},\mathrm{P}'), \] which is possible, by (3) and (4), if and only if \(c=0\). The set of associative P's is principal homogeneous under \(\mathrm{Z}^2(Y,M)\), again by (3) and (4). The set of associative P's up to equivalence is principal homogeneous under \(\mathrm{H}^2(Y,M)\) by (e).

pages.title: 2. Infinitesimal extensions of a morphism of group schemes

blocks.html: 2. Infinitesimal extensions of a morphism of group schemes

Let us resume the notation of Section 0. Let Y and X be two S-group functors. Let M be the kernel of the group morphism \(p_X : X \to X^+\). We thus have an exact sequence of S-group functors \[ 1 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow X \xrightarrow{\ p_X\ } X^+. \] By definition of \(X^+\), we have isomorphisms \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X^+) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{S_J}(Y_J,X_J), \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(Y,X^+) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{S_J\text{-gr.}}(Y_J,X_J), \] the morphism \(\mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,p_X) : \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X) \to \mathrm{Hom}_S(Y,X^+)\) associating with an S-morphism \(f : Y \to X\) the S-morphism \(f^+ : Y \to X^+\) corresponding, under the preceding isomorphisms, to the \(S_J\)-morphism \(f_J : Y \to X\) obtained from \(f\) by base change. If M is commutative, we can apply Proposition 1.2.4 to this situation.

In practice, we shall be interested in the following case: Y is flat over S, X is either representable (case (a)), or of the form \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(E)\) where E is representable (case (b)). The corresponding S-group functors M were computed in 0.9 (resp. 0.11), the actions of the inner automorphisms of X on M in 0.10 (resp. 0.12). On the other hand, Y being assumed flat, we may by 1.1.1 content ourselves with knowing the values of M on the S-schemes flat over S and the action of X on M when we regard X and M as functors on the category of S-schemes flat over S. Finally, in case (a), M is (on this category) of the form \(\prod_{S_0/S} N\) where N is a certain \(S_0\)-functor in abelian groups (0.5); we shall therefore have by 1.1.2 isomorphisms \(\mathrm{H}^i(Y,M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{H}^i(Y_0,N)\).
Applying 1.2.4, we obtain the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let S be a scheme, \(I\) and \(J\) two quasi-coherent ideals such that \(I \supset J\) and \(I\cdot J = 0\), defining the closed subschemes \(S_0\) and \(S_J\). For every S-functor Z, let \(Z_0\) and \(Z_J\) be the functors obtained by base change. Let X be an S-group functor of one of the following two kinds:

a) X is an S-group scheme,

b) \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(E)\) where E is an S-scheme, of finite presentation over S.

Let

a) \(\mathrm{L}_0\) be the \(S_0\)-functor in commutative groups defined by \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(T,\mathrm{L}_0) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_T}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_T, J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_T) \] on which \(X_0\) acts via its adjoint representation on \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\).

b) L be the functor in abelian groups on the category of S-schemes flat over S defined by \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(T,\mathrm{L}) = \Gamma(T, f_*(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_E}(\Omega^1_{E/S}, J\mathscr{O}_E))\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_T) \] (\(f\) denotes the structural morphism \(E \to S\)), on which X, considered as a functor on the same category, acts as we saw in 0.12.

Let Y be an S-group scheme flat over S and \(f_J : Y_J \to X_J\) a morphism of \(S_J\)-groups. In case (a), let \(Y_0\) act on \(\mathrm{L}_0\) through \(f_0 : Y_0 \to X_0\). Then:

(i) For \(f_J\) to lift to a morphism of S-groups \(Y \to X\), it is necessary and sufficient that the two following conditions be satisfied:

\((\mathrm{i}_1)\) \(f_J\) lifts to a morphism of S-functors \(Y \to X\), which in case (b) allows one to let Y act on L through the lifting \(Y \to X\) (an action which, as we have seen, is independent of the lifting chosen).

\((\mathrm{i}_2)\) A certain obstruction \(c(f_J)\), canonically defined by \(f_J\), is zero, where \(c(f_J)\) is a class in (a) \(\mathrm{H}^2(Y_0,\mathrm{L}_0)\), (b) \(\mathrm{H}^2(Y,\mathrm{L})\).

(ii) If the conditions of (i) are satisfied, the set of morphisms of S-group functors \(Y \to X\) extending \(f_J\), modulo the action of the inner automorphisms of X defined by the sections of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\) over \(S_J\), is principal homogeneous under (a) \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathrm{L}_0)\), (b) \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y,\mathrm{L})\).

(iii) If \(f : Y \to X\) is a morphism of S-group functors extending \(f_J\), the set of morphisms \(Y \to X\) transformed from \(f\) by the inner automorphisms defined by the sections of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\) over \(S_J\), is isomorphic to (a) \(\Gamma(\mathrm{L}_0)/\mathrm{H}^0(Y_0,\mathrm{L}_0)\), (b) \(\Gamma(\mathrm{L})/\mathrm{H}^0(Y,\mathrm{L})\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.2

Suppose moreover, in (a), that X is locally of finite presentation over S. Applying then 0.6, we obtain
a) \(\mathrm{H}^i(Y_0,\mathrm{L}_0) = \mathrm{H}^i(Y_0, \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J))\),
b) \(\mathrm{H}^i(Y,\mathrm{L}) = \mathrm{H}^i(Y, f_*(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_E}(\Omega^1_{E/S}, J\mathscr{O}_E)))\),
the modules whose cohomology we take being quasi-coherent.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.3

By 0.16 and 0.17, condition \((\mathrm{i}_1)\) is automatically satisfied when
a) X is smooth over S and Y is affine;
b) E is smooth over S, affine over S and Y is affine.
Moreover, under these conditions, one can write in case (a) \[ \mathrm{H}^i(Y_0,\mathrm{L}_0) = \mathrm{H}^i(Y_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J). \]

Let us now state a certain number of corollaries concerning the case where Y is a diagonalizable group (I, 4.4); we then know (loc. cit. 5.3.3) that if S is affine, the cohomology of Y with values in any quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module is zero. First a special case:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

Let S be a scheme and \(S_0\) a closed subscheme defined by a nilpotent ideal. Let Y be a diagonalizable S-group and let:

a) X be an S-group locally of finite presentation over S,

b) \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(E)\) where E is an S-scheme locally of finite presentation.

Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of S-groups such that the morphism \(f_0 : Y_0 \to X_0\) obtained by base change is the unit morphism. Then \(f\) is the unit morphism.

Indeed, the question is local on S and (in (b)) on E. We may therefore suppose S affine and (in (b)) E of finite presentation over S. Introducing now the closed subschemes \(S_n\) of S defined by the powers of the ideal defining \(S_0\), we are reduced to the case where \(S_0\) is defined by an ideal of square zero, and in this case the stated assertion follows from the theorem, via 2.2.
In the case where one does not necessarily suppose that \(f_0\) is the unit morphism, we have:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

Let S and \(S_0\) be as in 2.4. Suppose moreover S affine. Let Y be a diagonalizable S-group, X an S-group functor and \(f_0 : Y_0 \to X_0\) a morphism of \(S_0\)-group functors.

(i) Suppose we have one of the two following properties: (a) X is an S-group locally of finite presentation over S; (b) \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(E)\) where E is of finite presentation over S. Then \(f_0\) extends to a morphism of S-groups \(Y \to X\) if and only if it extends to a morphism of S-functors \(Y \to X\).

(ii) Suppose we have one of the two following properties: (a) X is an S-group smooth over S; (b) \(X = \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(E)\) where E is smooth and affine over S. Then \(f_0\) extends to a morphism of S-groups \(Y \to X\), and two such extensions are conjugate by an inner automorphism of X defined by a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_0\) over \(S_0\).

Introducing the \(S_n\) as above, (i) follows step by step from part (ii) of the theorem. For (ii), one reasons in the same way, remarking that the conditions of 2.1 (i) are automatically satisfied by 2.3 (note that a scheme smooth over S is necessarily locally of finite presentation over S, hence a scheme smooth and affine over S is necessarily of finite presentation); moreover every section of \(X_{S_n}\) over \(S_n\) lifts to a section of \(X_{S_{n+1}}\) over \(S_{n+1}\) (by the definition of "smooth over S" for (a), and by 0.16 for (b)); if \(f\) and \(f'\) are two liftings of \(f_0\), one may suppose step by step that \(f_n = f'_n\) by lifting the inner automorphism whose existence is asserted by part (ii) of the theorem, which completes the proof.
Reasoning in the same way, we obtain, taking account of Remark 2.3:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.6

Let S be a scheme, \(I\) a nilpotent ideal defining the closed subscheme \(S_0\), Y an S-group flat over S and affine, X an S-group smooth over S.

(i) If, for every \(n \geqslant 0\), we have \(\mathrm{H}^2(Y_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} I^{n+1}/I^{n+2}) = 0\), every morphism of \(S_0\)-groups \(f_0 : Y_0 \to X_0\) lifts to a morphism of S-groups \(f : Y \to X\).

(ii) If, for every \(n \geqslant 0\), we have \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} I^{n+1}/I^{n+2}) = 0\), two such liftings are conjugate by an inner automorphism of X defined by a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_0\) over \(S_0\).
Now we have the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.7

Let S be an affine scheme, G an affine S-group, \(\mathscr{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module, \(\mathscr{L}\) a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module. Suppose we have an action of G on \(\mathscr{F}\) in the sense of Exposé I, which defines an action of G on \(\mathscr{F}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{L}\). Denote by A the ring of S, and by L the A-module defining \(\mathscr{L}\) (which is therefore a projective module). There is a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{H}^*(G, \mathscr{F}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{L}) \simeq \mathrm{H}^*(G,\mathscr{F})\otimes_A \mathrm{L}. \]

Indeed, in the situation of the statement, the cohomology groups of G with values in \(\mathscr{F}\) are computed as the groups of sections of the cohomology sheaves \(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^n(G,\mathscr{F})\) (I, 5.3). Now it follows immediately from the definition of these cohomology sheaves that their formation commutes with tensor product by a locally free sheaf. Taking sections (we are over an affine base), we find the announced result.
Using the lemma, one transforms 2.6 into:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.8

Let S be an affine scheme, \(I\) a nilpotent ideal on S defining the closed subscheme \(S_0\). Suppose the \(I^{n+1}/I^{n+2}\) locally free on \(S_0\). Let Y be an S-group flat over S and affine, X an S-group smooth over S, and \(f_0 : Y_0 \to X_0\) a morphism of \(S_0\)-groups.

(i) If \(\mathrm{H}^2(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)) = 0\), \(f_0\) lifts to a morphism of S-groups \(Y \to X\).

(ii) If \(\mathrm{H}^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)) = 0\), two such liftings are conjugate by an inner automorphism of X defined by a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_0\) over \(S_0\).
In particular, taking \(Y = X\):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.9

Let S and \(S_0\) be as above. Let X be an S-group smooth over S and affine.

(i) If \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)) = 0\), every endomorphism of X over S inducing the identity on \(X_0\) is the inner automorphism defined by a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_0\) over \(S_0\).

(ii) If \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)) = 0\), every \(S_0\)-automorphism of \(X_0\) extends to an S-automorphism of X.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.10

The assertions concerning the \(\mathrm{H}^1\) have converses, by the theorem. Let us mention the following one as an example: if \(S = I_{S_0}\) is the scheme of dual numbers over \(S_0\) (II, 2.1) and if X is a flat S-group such that every automorphism of X over S inducing the identity on \(S_0\) is the inner automorphism defined by a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_0\) over \(S_0\), then \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)) = 0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.11

Let S, \(I\) and \(J\) be as in 2.1. Let Y be an S-group scheme flat over S, X an S-group scheme, and \(f : Y \to X\) a morphism of S-groups. The set of morphisms from Y to X deduced from \(f\) by conjugation by those \(x \in X(S)\) inducing the unit of \(X(S_J)\) is isomorphic to the quotient \[ \mathrm{E} = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J)\big/\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)}, \] where the second group consists of the \(\mathscr{O}_{S_0}\)-morphisms \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0} \to J\) which, by every base change \(S' \to S_0\), give morphisms \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{S'} \to J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{S'}\) invariant under the action of \(Y_0(S')\) on the first factor.

By 2.1 (iii), we know that the set sought is isomorphic to \(\Gamma(\mathrm{L}_0)/\mathrm{H}^0(Y_0,\mathrm{L}_0)\). Now \(\Gamma(\mathrm{L}_0) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0},J)\) and \(\mathrm{H}^0(Y_0,\mathrm{L}_0)\) is obviously none other than \(\Gamma(\mathrm{L}_0)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)}\) in the sense of the preceding statement.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.12

Under the conditions of 2.11, suppose moreover \(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\) locally free (of finite type). Then \[ \mathrm{E} \simeq \Gamma(S_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J)\big/\mathrm{H}^0(Y_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J). \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.13

Suppose moreover \(Y_0\) diagonalizable. Then \[ \mathrm{E} \simeq \Gamma(S_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J)\big/\Gamma(S_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J) \] where \(\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)}\) may be constructed as the factor of the decomposition of I, 4.7.3 corresponding to the zero character of \(Y_0\). Indeed, if \(Y_0 \simeq \mathbf{D}_{S_0}(M)\), we have by loc. cit. a direct sum decomposition: \[ \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0) = \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)_0 \oplus \coprod_{\substack{m\in M\\ m\neq 0}} \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)_m. \] Tensoring with \(J\), we find an analogous decomposition for \(\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J\), whence the relation \[ \mathrm{H}^0(Y_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes J) \simeq \Gamma(S_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)_0 \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J). \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.14

Suppose moreover \(S_0\) affine. Then \[ \mathrm{E} \simeq \Gamma\Bigl(S_0, \bigl[\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)/\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)}\bigr]\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J\Bigr). \]

pages.title: 3. Infinitesimal extensions of a group scheme

blocks.html: 3. Infinitesimal extensions of a group scheme

Still in the notation of Section 0 (S, \(I\), \(J\), etc.), let us give ourselves an S-scheme X and suppose \(X_J\) equipped with a group structure. We propose to find the S-group structures on X inducing the given structure on \(X_J\).

From now on, we suppose X flat over S. Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be the category of S-schemes flat over S. We thus have \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}\). We shall denote by Y, resp. M, the functor on \(\mathscr{C}\) defined by \(X^+\), resp. \(\mathrm{L}'_X\). The canonical morphism \(p_X : X \to X^+\) defines a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) \[ p : X \longrightarrow Y \] and the action of \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) on X in \(\widehat{(\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}}\) defines an action of M on X in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). One verifies at once that X does thus become formally principal homogeneous under \(M_Y\) over Y (cf. 0.2 (i) and 0.4).

The action of \(X^+\) on \(\mathrm{L}'_X\) defined in 0.8 (denoted \(\mathrm{Ad}\) in loc. cit.) defines an action, denoted \(f\), of Y on M. We know, on the other hand (0.8), that \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}}(Z,M) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(Z_0, \mathrm{L}_0), \qquad Z\in \mathrm{Ob}\,\mathscr{C}, \] where \(\mathrm{L}_0\) is the functor defined in 0.5.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1

(i) Condition \((+)_n\) of 1.3 is satisfied for every positive integer \(n\).

(ii) If we let the \(S_0\)-group \(X_0\) act on the \(S_0\)-functor \(\mathrm{L}_0\) through its adjoint representation, there is a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{H}^*(X_0,\mathrm{L}_0) \simeq \mathrm{H}^*(Y,M), \] (the first cohomology is computed in \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S_0}\), the second in \(\mathscr{C}\)).

The two parts of the lemma follow from the relation: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}}(Y,M) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{(\mathrm{Sch})_{/S_0}}}(X^+\underset{S}{\times}S_0, \mathrm{L}_0) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(X_0,\mathrm{L}_0) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}}(X,M) \] which comes at once from the definition of M as a \(\prod_{S_0/S}\). This relation being more generally satisfied with X, Y replaced by \(X^n\), \(Y^n\), we deduce that every morphism \(X^n \to M\) factors in a unique way through \(Y^n\), which implies \((+)_n\). We also deduce the relation \(\mathrm{C}^*(Y,M) = \mathrm{C}^*(X_0,\mathrm{L}_0)\), which implies (ii).
We may therefore apply the constructions of 1.3. In particular:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2

Let \(\mathrm{P} : X\times_S X \to X\) be a morphism. For P to lift the group law of \(X_J\), it is necessary and sufficient that P be an admissible composition law on X.

Indeed, for P to lift the group law of \(X_J\), it is necessary and sufficient that P lift the group law of \(X^+\), or again that of Y. It thus only remains to show that every morphism P lifting the group law of \(X_J\) satisfies the identity \((++)\) of 1.3.2, which is exactly what we saw in 0.8.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3

Let S be a scheme and \(S_0\) a closed subscheme defined by a nilpotent ideal. Let X be an S-scheme, flat, and quasi-compact or locally of finite presentation over S. Let \(\mathrm{P} : X\times_S X \to X\) be a composition law on X. For P to be a group law, it is necessary and sufficient that the two following conditions be satisfied:

(i) P is associative.

(ii) P induces on \(X_0 = X\times_S S_0\) a group law.

These conditions are obviously necessary. Let us show that they are sufficient. Suppose first that X has a section over S. As \(X(S')\) is then non-empty for each \(S' \to S\), it suffices (Bourbaki, Algèbre, Ch. I, exercise 2, b)) to show that every translation by an element of \(X(S')\) is an isomorphism. We may obviously suppose \(S' = S\); this translation induces on \(X_0\) a translation, which is therefore an automorphism of \(X_0\). One concludes by flatness (SGA 1 III 4.2).

No longer supposing now that X has a section over S, suppose there exists an \(S' \to S\) such that \(X_{S'}\) has a section over \(S'\). Then \(X_{S'}\) is an \(S'\)-group by what we have just seen; consider its unit section \(e'\). The inverse image of \(e'\) under \(\mathrm{pr}_i : S'\times_S S' \to S'\) (\(i=1,2\)) is the unit section of \(X_{S''}\) for the group law inverse image of \(\mathrm{P}_{S'}\) under \(\mathrm{pr}_i\). But as P is "defined over S", these two group laws coincide, hence so do their unit sections. We thus have \(\mathrm{pr}_1^*(e') = \mathrm{pr}_2^*(e')\). If \(S' \to S\) is a descent morphism, there will exist a section of X giving \(e'\) by extension of the base, and we shall be done. As \(X\times_S X\) has a section over X (the diagonal section), we see that it now suffices to prove that \(X \to S\) is a descent morphism. Now it is flat and surjective, and quasi-compact or locally of finite presentation, hence covering for (fpqc), hence a descent morphism (Exposé IV, no. 6).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

In fact the hypothesis \(X \to S\) quasi-compact or locally of finite presentation is superfluous, by virtue of the following result, which the reader will prove as an exercise on Exposé IV: Under the conditions of the text on S and \(S_0\), if \(X \to S\) is a flat morphism and \(X_0 \to S_0\) a morphism covering for (fpqc), then \(X \to S\) is a descent morphism.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4

For two admissible composition laws on X to be equivalent, it is necessary and sufficient that they be deduced from one another by an automorphism of X over S inducing the identity on \(X_J\).

Indeed, the morphisms constructed in 1.3.1 are exactly those of the preceding statement (by 0.7).
Taking account of all the preceding results, Proposition 1.3.6 gives:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.5

Let S be a scheme, \(I\) and \(J\) two ideals on S such that \(I \supset J\), \(I\cdot J = 0\), and \(S_0\) and \(S_J\) the closed subschemes of S they define. Let X be an S-scheme flat over S (and locally of finite presentation or quasi-compact over S), and \(X_0\) and \(X_J\) the schemes obtained by base change. Suppose \(X_J\) equipped with a structure of \(S_J\)-group and denote by \(\mathrm{L}_0\) the \(S_0\)-functor in abelian groups defined by the formula \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(T,\mathrm{L}_0) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_T}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_T, J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_T) \] on which \(X_0\) acts through its adjoint representation.

(i) For there to exist a structure of S-group on X inducing the given structure on \(X_J\), it is necessary and sufficient that the following conditions be satisfied:

\((\mathrm{i}_1)\) There exists a morphism of S-schemes \(X\times_S X \to X\) inducing the group law of \(X_J\).

\((\mathrm{i}_2)\) A certain obstruction belonging to \(\mathrm{H}^3(X_0,\mathrm{L}_0)\) (canonically defined by the data of X and of the group law of \(X_J\)) is zero.

(ii) If the conditions of (i) are satisfied, the set of group laws on X inducing the given law on \(X_J\), modulo the S-automorphisms of X inducing the identity on \(X_J\), is a principal homogeneous set under \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathrm{L}_0)\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.6

Let \(X_J\) be an \(S_J\)-scheme smooth over \(S_J\) and affine. By 0.15, there exists, up to isomorphism, a unique S-scheme X, smooth over S, reducing to \(X_J\). If \(X_J\) is equipped with a structure of \(S_J\)-group, it follows from 0.16 that condition \((\mathrm{i}_1)\) is automatically satisfied. Moreover, by 0.6 the definition of \(\mathrm{L}_0\) simplifies and we obtain:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

Let S, \(I\) and \(J\) be as in 3.1. Let \(X_J\) be an \(S_J\)-group smooth over \(S_J\) and affine.

(i) The set of S-groups smooth over S and reducing to \(X_J\), up to isomorphism (inducing the identity on \(X_J\)), is empty or principal homogeneous under the group \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J)\).

(ii) There exists an S-group smooth over S reducing to \(X_J\) if and only if a certain obstruction in \(\mathrm{H}^3(X_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} J)\) is zero.
From this one deduces as usual the following corollaries:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.8

Let S be a scheme and \(S_0\) a closed subscheme defined by a nilpotent ideal \(I\). Let \(X_0\) be an \(S_0\)-group smooth over S and affine.

(i) If \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} I^{n+1}/I^{n+2}) = 0\) for every \(n \geqslant 0\), two S-groups smooth over S reducing to \(X_0\) are isomorphic (by an isomorphism inducing the identity on \(X_0\)).

(ii) If \(\mathrm{H}^3(X_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} I^{n+1}/I^{n+2}) = 0\) for every \(n \geqslant 0\), there exists an S-group smooth over S, reducing to \(X_0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.9

Let S be an affine scheme and \(S_0\) a closed subscheme defined by a nilpotent ideal \(I\). Suppose the \(I^{n+1}/I^{n+2}\) locally free on \(S_0\). Let \(X_0\) be an \(S_0\)-group smooth and affine over \(S_0\).

(i) If \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)) = 0\), two S-groups smooth over S reducing to \(X_0\) are isomorphic.

(ii) If \(\mathrm{H}^3(X_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)) = 0\), there exists an S-group smooth over S reducing to \(X_0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.10

Let \(S_0\) be a scheme and \(S = I_{S_0}\) the scheme of dual numbers over \(S_0\). Let \(X_0\) be an \(S_0\)-group smooth over \(S_0\). In order that every S-group Y, smooth over S, such that Y is \(S_0\)-isomorphic to \(X_0\), be S-isomorphic to \(X = X_0\times_{S_0} S\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)) = 0\).

Indeed, by virtue of 3.5 the set of classes, up to an isomorphism of S-groups "inducing the identity on \(X_0\)", of such groups Y, is in one-to-one correspondence with \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0))\), hence the set of classes, up to an arbitrary isomorphism of S-groups, is in one-to-one correspondence with \(\mathrm{H}^2(X_0,\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0))/\Gamma_0\), where \(\Gamma_0 = \mathrm{Aut}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(X_0)\) (which acts in the obvious way on the \(\mathrm{H}^2\)). The conclusion follows at once from this.
4. Infinitesimal extensions of closed subgroups
Let us first state a result valid in an arbitrary abelian category.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.1

Let \[ 0 \longrightarrow A' \xrightarrow{\ i\ } A \xrightarrow{\ p\ } A'' \longrightarrow 0 \] be an exact sequence, \(\phi : A' \to Q\) a morphism and \(\pi : A'' \to P\) an epimorphism with kernel C. Let E be the set (up to isomorphism) of quadruples \((\mathrm{B},f,g,h)\) such that the sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow Q \xrightarrow{\ f\ } \mathrm{B} \xrightarrow{\ g\ } P \longrightarrow 0 \] is exact and the diagram below commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & A' \ar[r]^-{i} \ar[d]_-{\phi} & A \ar[r]^-{p} \ar[d]^-{h} & A'' \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{\pi} & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & Q \ar[r]_-{f} & \mathrm{B} \ar[r]_-{g} & P \ar[r] & 0 } \]

(i) For E to be non-empty, it is necessary and sufficient that the image in \(\mathrm{Ext}^1(\mathrm{C},Q)\) of the element A of \(\mathrm{Ext}^1(A'',A')\) be zero.

(ii) Under these conditions, E is a principal homogeneous set under the abelian group \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm{C},Q)\).

Let us introduce the amalgamated sum \(\mathrm{B}' = A\coprod_{A'}Q\). We then have a commutative diagram in which the rows are exact: \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & A' \ar[r]^-{i} \ar[d]_-{\phi} & A \ar[r]^-{p} \ar[d] & A'' \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{=} & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & Q \ar[r]_-{j} & \mathrm{B}' \ar[r] & A'' \ar[r] & 0 } \] and it is clear that the category of solutions of the problem posed is canonically isomorphic to the category of solutions of the corresponding problem for the sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow Q \longrightarrow \mathrm{B}' \longrightarrow A'' \longrightarrow 0 \] and the morphisms \(\mathrm{id}_Q\) and \(\pi : A'' \to P\). We are thus reduced to proving the lemma in the case where \(A' \to Q\) is an isomorphism. In that case, the set E is obviously in one-to-one correspondence with the set of subobjects N of A such that \(A \to A''\) induces an isomorphism of N with the kernel C of \(A'' \to P\), that is, the set of morphisms \(\mathrm{C} \to A\) lifting the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{C}\to A''\); which gives the desired result.
From (ii) one deduces immediately:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2

Let \((\mathrm{X},\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X})\) be a ringed space, \(\mathscr{J}\) an ideal of square zero of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}\), and \(\mathscr{F}\) an \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}\)-module. Set \(\mathscr{F}_0 = \mathscr{F}/\mathscr{J}\mathscr{F}\) and let \(\mathscr{G}_0\) be a quotient module of \(\mathscr{F}_0\). Let us give ourselves a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}/\mathscr{J}\)-modules \[ f : \mathscr{J}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}/\mathscr{J}}\mathscr{G}_0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{Q}. \]

Let \(\mathscr{E}\) be the sheaf on X defined as follows: for each open set U of X, \(\mathscr{E}(\mathrm U)\) is the set of quotient modules \(\mathscr{G}\) of \(\mathscr{F}|_{\mathrm U}\) such that \(\mathscr{G}/(\mathscr{J}|_{\mathrm U})\mathscr{G} = \mathscr{G}_0|_{\mathrm U}\) and such that there exists an isomorphism (obviously unique) \[ (\mathscr{J}|_{\mathrm U})\mathscr{G} \simeq \mathscr{Q}|_{\mathrm U} \] making the following diagram commutative \[ \xymatrix{ (\mathscr{J}|_{\mathrm U}) \otimes (\mathscr{G}_0|_{\mathrm U}) \ar[r]^-{f|_{\mathrm U}} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{can.}} & \mathscr{Q}|_{\mathrm U} \\ (\mathscr{J}|_{\mathrm U})\mathscr{G} \ar[ur]_-{\simeq} & } \]

Then \(\mathscr{E}\) is a sheaf formally principal homogeneous under the sheaf of commutative groups \[ \mathscr{A} = \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}/\mathscr{J}}(\mathscr{H}_0,\mathscr{Q}), \] where we have set \(\mathscr{G}_0 = \mathscr{F}_0/\mathscr{H}_0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3

(TDTE IV 5.1) Let S be a scheme, \(S_0\) a closed subscheme defined by the quasi-coherent ideal \(J\) of square zero, X an S-scheme, \(\mathscr{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}\)-module, \(X_0 = X\times_S S_0\), \(\mathscr{F}_0 = \mathscr{F}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_{S_0}\). Let \(\mathscr{G}_0 = \mathscr{F}_0/\mathscr{H}_0\) be a quasi-coherent quotient module of \(\mathscr{F}_0\), flat over \(S_0\). For every open set U of X, let \(\mathbf{E}(\mathrm U)\) be the set of quotient modules \(\mathscr{G}\) of the \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm U}\)-module \(\mathscr{F}|_{\mathrm U}\), flat over S and such that \(\mathscr{G}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_{S_0} \simeq \mathscr{G}_0|_{\mathrm U}\) (which are necessarily quasi-coherent, being extensions of two quasi-coherent modules, cf. EGA III, 1.4.17). Then the \(\mathbf{E}(\mathrm U)\) form a sheaf \(\mathbf{E}\) on X, which is formally principal homogeneous under the sheaf of commutative groups \[ \mathscr{A} = \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\mathscr{H}_0, \mathscr{G}_0\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}J). \]

By the fundamental criterion of flatness (see for example SGA 1 IV, 5.1), \(\mathscr{G}\) is flat over S if and only if \(\mathscr{G}_0\) is flat over \(S_0\) and the canonical morphism \(J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{G}_0 \to J\mathscr{G}\) is an isomorphism. It thus suffices to apply the preceding proposition, taking for \(f\) the identity morphism of \(J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{G}_0 = J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}}\mathscr{G}_0\).
Taking now \(\mathscr{F} = \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}\) and reasoning as usual, one deduces from this:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

Let S, \(S_0\), \(J\), X, \(X_0\) be as above. Let \(Y_0\) be a closed subscheme of \(X_0\), flat over \(S_0\). Let E be the set of closed subschemes Y of X, flat over S, such that \(Y\times_S S_0 = Y_0\).

(i) The set E is empty or principal homogeneous under \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\mathscr{I}_{Y_0}, \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}J) \] where \(\mathscr{I}_{Y_0}\) is the Ideal defining \(Y_0\) in \(X_0\).

(ii) For E to be non-empty, it is necessary and sufficient that the two following conditions be satisfied:

(a) There exists locally on X a solution of the problem.

(b) A certain obstruction is zero, which lies in \[ \mathrm{H}^1(X_0, \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\mathscr{I}_{Y_0}, \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}J)). \]

Let us now carry out a certain number of transformations. As \(J\) is of square zero, we may also write \[ \mathscr{A}_0 = \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\mathscr{I}_{Y_0}, \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}J) = \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}(\mathscr{I}_{Y_0}/\mathscr{I}_{Y_0}^2, \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}J). \] As \(\mathscr{I}_{Y_0}/\mathscr{I}_{Y_0}^2\) is annihilated by \(\mathscr{I}_{Y_0}\), we may regard it as a sheaf on \(Y_0\), which we denote by \(\mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0}\), the conormal sheaf of \(Y_0\) in \(X_0\). Setting \[ \mathscr{A}' = \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0}, \mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}J), \] we shall have \(\mathrm{H}^i(X_0,\mathscr{A}) = \mathrm{H}^i(Y_0,\mathscr{A}')\), \(i\geqslant 0\).
Introducing now two ideals \(I\) and \(J\), reasoning as in 0.2, and removing in the usual manner the hypothesis "Y closed", we obtain

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5

Let S be a scheme, \(S_0\) and \(S_J\) the closed subschemes defined by the quasi-coherent ideals \(I\) and \(J\), such that \(I\supset J\) and \(I\cdot J = 0\). Let X be an S-scheme and \(Y_J\) a subscheme of \(X_J\), flat over \(S_J\). Let \(\mathscr{A}_0\) be the \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\)-module defined by \[ \mathscr{A}_0 = \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\mathscr{N}_{Y_J/X_J}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_J}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}, J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}). \]

(i) For there to exist a subscheme Y of X, reducing to \(Y_J\), flat over S, it is necessary and sufficient that the following conditions be satisfied:

(a) Such a Y exists locally on X.

(b) A certain obstruction in \(\mathrm{R}^1\Gamma(Y_0,\mathscr{A}_0)\) is zero.

(ii) Under these conditions, the set of Y satisfying the required conditions is principal homogeneous under the commutative group \(\Gamma(Y_0,\mathscr{A}_0)\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.6

If X is flat over S and if \(Y_J\) is locally a complete intersection in \(X_J\), then condition (a) is always satisfied, and every S-flat Y lifting \(Y_J\) is then locally a complete intersection in X. If moreover \(Y_0\) is affine, condition (b) is also satisfied.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.7

It follows from 4.5 (ii) that, for every pair \((Y,Y')\) of closed subschemes of X, flat over S and reducing to \(Y_J\), there is given a deviation \[ d(Y,Y') \in \mathrm{H}^0(Y_0,\mathscr{A}_0) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\mathscr{N}_{Y_J/X_J}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_J}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}, J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.8

Let S, \(I\), \(J\) and X be as in 4.5, with \(I\) nilpotent. Let Y be a subscheme of X.

(i) Let T be an S-scheme and \(f : \mathrm{T}\to X\) an S-morphism such that \(f_J : \mathrm{T}_J \to X_J\) factors through \(Y_J\). One can then define an obstruction \[ c(X,Y,f) \in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(f_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}), J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}) \] whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of a factorization of \(f\) through Y.

(ii) Suppose in particular Y flat over S and let \(Y'\) be a subscheme of X, flat over S, such that \(Y'_J = Y_J\). Consider the canonical morphism \[ u : \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\mathscr{N}_{Y_J/X_J}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_J}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}, J\mathscr{O}_{Y}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}, J\mathscr{O}_{Y}). \] If we denote by \(i' : Y' \to X\) the canonical immersion, we have \[ c(X,Y,i') = u(d(Y,Y')), \] where \(d(Y,Y')\) is the deviation of 4.7.

(iii) Consider the canonical morphism (SGA 1 II, formula 4.3) \[ \mathscr{N}_{Y/X} \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm D\ } \Omega^1_{X/S}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}}\mathscr{O}_Y \] It induces, for every \(f_0 : \mathrm T_0 \to X_0\) factoring through \(Y_0\), a morphism: \[ v_{f_0} : \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(f_0^*(\Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}), J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(f_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}), J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}), \] (The left-hand side is none other than \(\mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(\mathrm T,\mathrm{L}_X)\), cf. 0.2). Let \(a \in \mathrm{Hom}_{X^+}(\mathrm T,\mathrm{L}_X)\); consider the composite morphism \[ g : \mathrm T \xrightarrow{\ a\times f\ } \mathrm{L}_X\underset{X^+}{\times}X \longrightarrow X, \] where \(f : \mathrm T \to X\) is a morphism of the kind studied in (i). Then \[ c(X,Y,g) - c(X,Y,f) = v_{f_0}(a). \]

We shall prove part (i) of the proposition, leaving to the reader the care of (not) verifying assertions (ii) and (iii); this verification is done by reduction to the affine case, then by comparison of the explicit definitions.

Let us then prove (i). As T, \(\mathrm T_J\) and \(\mathrm T_0\) on the one hand, and X, \(X_J\) and \(X_0\) on the other, have the same underlying topological space, the continuous maps underlying \(f\), \(f_J\) and \(f_0\) are the same. Denote by \(f(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}) = f_0(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T})\) the (set-theoretic) direct image of the sheaf of rings \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}\) by the preceding continuous map. The morphism \(f : \mathrm T \to X\) defines a morphism of sheaves of rings \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X}\to f(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T})\).

As usual, we may restrict to the case where Y is closed, hence defined by a sheaf of ideals \(\mathscr{I}_Y\). For \(f\) to factor through Y, it is necessary and sufficient that the composite map \(\mathscr{I}_Y \to \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X} \to f(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T})\) be zero. As \(f_J\) factors through \(Y_J\), the composite map \(\mathscr{I}_{Y_J} \to \mathscr{O}_{X_J} \to f(\mathscr{O}_{T_J})\) is zero. Consider the commutative diagram in which the first row is exact \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & f(J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}) \ar[r] & f(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}) \ar[r] & f(\mathscr{O}_{T_J}) \\ & \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm X} \ar[u] \ar[r] & \mathscr{O}_{X_J} \ar[u] \\ & \mathscr{I}_Y \ar[u] \ar[ur]^-{h} \ar[r] & \mathscr{I}_{Y_J} \ar[u] } \] it is completed by a morphism \[ h : \mathscr{I}_Y \longrightarrow f(J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}). \] But, as \(J^2=0\) and \(IJ=0\), we have a factorization \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{I}_Y \ar[rr]^-{h} \ar[dr]_-{\mathrm{surj.}} & & f(J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}) = f^0(J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}) \\ & \mathscr{I}_Y/\mathscr{I}_Y^2\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0} \ar[ur]_-{h'} & } \] By definition of the direct image, \(h'\) defines a morphism \[ f_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}) \longrightarrow J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T} \] which is the desired obstruction \(c(X,Y,f)\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.9

This obstruction is computed locally on T.
