If we suppose T affine, say \(\mathrm T = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathrm C\), and likewise \(X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathrm B\), \(Y = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathrm B/\mathrm I_Y\), \(S = \mathrm{Spec}\,A\), \(S_J = \mathrm{Spec}\,A/J\), \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}\,A/I\), it is computed by the following commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm I_Y \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm B \ar[r]^-{f} & \mathrm C \ar[d]^-{c} \\ \mathrm I_Y/\mathrm I_Y^2 \ar[r] & \mathrm I_Y/\mathrm I_Y^2\otimes_{\mathrm B}\mathrm C_0 \ar[r]_-{c} & J\mathrm C } \] The obstruction \(c\) is thus defined as the unique morphism of \(\mathrm C_0\)-modules \[ \mathrm I_Y/\mathrm I_Y^2\otimes_{\mathrm B}\mathrm C_0 \longrightarrow J\mathrm C \] such that, with the obvious notation, we have \(c(\overline x\otimes 1) = f(x)\) for every \(x\in \mathrm I_Y\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.10

If \(Y_0\) is locally a complete intersection in \(X_0\) (which implies that Y is one in X), we have \(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0} = \mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0}\). More precisely, we have in any case a surjective morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\)-Modules \[ \mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0} \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{surj.}\ } \mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0} \] which is also injective if Y is locally a complete intersection in X (as follows at once from the fact that Y and \(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\) are flat over S). In the "affine" case of the preceding remark, this translates into a morphism \[ \mathrm I_Y/\mathrm I_Y^2\otimes_{\mathrm B}\mathrm B_0 \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{surj.}\ } \mathrm I_{Y_0}/\mathrm I_{Y_0}^2, \] hence a morphism \[ \mathrm I_Y/\mathrm I_Y^2\otimes_{\mathrm B}\mathrm C_0 \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{surj.}\ } \mathrm I_{Y_0}/\mathrm I_{Y_0}^2\otimes_{\mathrm B_0}\mathrm C_0 \] which is bijective if Y is locally a complete intersection in X.

We now propose to study the following situation: we have, as in 4.8, S, \(S_J\) and \(S_0\); we have two S-schemes X and \(X'\), a subscheme Y (resp. \(Y'\)) of X (resp. \(X'\)), an S-scheme T, and morphisms \(f : \mathrm T \to X'\) and \(g : X' \to X\). \[ \xymatrix{ & Y' \ar@{^{(}->}[d]^-{i'} & & Y \ar@{^{(}->}[d]^-{i} \\ \mathrm T \ar[r]_-{f} & X' \ar[rr]_-{g} & & X } \] We suppose that, upon reduction modulo \(J\), this diagram is completed into a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & Y'_J \ar@{-->}[rr] \ar@{^{(}->}[d]^-{i'_J} & & Y_J \ar@{^{(}->}[d]^-{i_J} \\ \mathrm T_J \ar[r]_-{f_J} & X'_J \ar[rr]_-{g_J} & & X_J } \] By 4.8, we thus have obstructions: \[ c(X,Y,g\circ i) \in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0'}}(i_0'^*g_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}), J\mathscr{O}_{Y'}), \] \[ c(X',Y',f) \in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(f_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y'/X'}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y'}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0'}), J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}), \] \[ c(X,Y,g\circ f) \in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(f_0^*g_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}), J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}), \] whose relations we seek to compute.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.12

(i) Suppose \(Y'\) flat over \(S'\). We then have a natural morphism \[ b_{f_0} : \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0'}}(i_0'^*g_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}), J\mathscr{O}_{Y'}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(f_0^*g_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}), J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}). \]

(ii) Suppose moreover \(Y_0'\) locally a complete intersection in \(X_0'\). We then have a natural morphism \[ a_{g_0} : \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(f_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y'/X'}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y'}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0'}), J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(f_0^*g_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}), J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}). \]

To construct \(b_{f_0}\), it suffices to exhibit an \(\mathscr{O}_{T_0}\)-morphism \[ f_0^*(J\mathscr{O}_{Y'}) \longrightarrow J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}\, ; \] in any case we have a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ f_0^*(J\mathscr{O}_{Y'}) \ar@{-->}[r] & J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T} \\ f^*(J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0'}) \ar[u] \ar[r]_-{\sim} & J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{T_0} \ar[u] } \] If \(Y'\) is flat over \(S'\), the first vertical arrow is an isomorphism, and we complete the square immediately.

In the same manner, one constructs \(a_{g_0}\) by exhibiting an \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0'}\)-morphism \[ g_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}) \longrightarrow \mathscr{N}_{Y'/X'}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y'}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0'}\, ; \] now in any case we have a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ g_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}) \ar@{-->}[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{N}_{Y'/X'}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y'}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0'} \\ g_0^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0}) \ar[r] & \mathscr{N}_{Y_0'/X_0'} \ar[u] } \] if \(Y_0'\) is locally a complete intersection in \(X_0'\), the second vertical arrow is an isomorphism and we complete the diagram.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.13

Suppose \(Y'\) flat over S and \(Y_0'\) locally a complete intersection in \(X_0'\). We then have the formula: \[ c(X,Y,g\circ f) = a_{g_0}(c(X',Y',f)) + b_{f_0}(c(X,Y,g\circ i')). \]

As the definition of the various obstructions and of the morphisms \(a_{g_0}\) and \(b_{f_0}\) is local, one sees easily that it suffices to verify the given formula when the various schemes involved are affine. So write \(S = \mathrm{Spec}\,\Lambda\), \(S_J = \mathrm{Spec}\,\Lambda/J\), \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}\,\Lambda/I\), \(\mathrm T = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathrm C\), \(X = \mathrm{Spec}\,A\), \(Y = \mathrm{Spec}\,A/\mathrm I_Y = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathrm B\), \(X' = \mathrm{Spec}\,A'\), \(Y' = \mathrm{Spec}\,A'/\mathrm I_{Y'} = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathrm B'\).

We thus have a diagram of rings and ideals \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathrm B' \ar[d]^-{\pi'} & \mathrm B \ar[d]^-{\pi} \\ \mathrm C & A' \ar[l]^-{f} & A \ar[l]_-{g} \\ & \mathrm I_{Y'} \ar[u] & \mathrm I_Y \ar[u] } \]

Let us study the various terms of the formula to be proved. First \(c = c(X,Y,g\circ f)\). We have seen that it is the unique \(\mathrm C_0\)-morphism \(\mathrm I_Y/\mathrm I_Y^2\otimes_{\mathrm B}\mathrm C_0 \to J\mathrm C\) such that \(c(\overline x\otimes 1) = f(g(x))\) for every \(x\in \mathrm I_Y\). So let \(x\in \mathrm I_Y\); set \(x' = g(x) \in \mathrm I_{Y'} + JA'\). Write \(x' = y' + \sum \lambda_i a_i'\), \(y'\in \mathrm I_{Y'}\), \(\lambda_i\in J\), \(a_i'\in A'\). We thus have \[ c(X,Y,g\circ f)(\overline x\otimes 1) = f(x') = f(y') + \sum \lambda_i f(a_i'). \]

Consider now \(a_{g_0}(c(X',Y',f))\). According to the definitions laid down, it is defined by the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm I_{Y'} \ar@{^{(}->}[r] \ar[d] & A' \ar[r]^-{f} & \mathrm C \\ \mathrm I_{Y'}/\mathrm I_{Y'}^2\otimes_{B'}\mathrm C_0 \ar[r]^-{\sim} & \mathrm I_{Y_0'}/\mathrm I_{Y_0'}^2\otimes_{B_0'}\mathrm C_0 \ar[d]^-{g_0} \ar[u] & \\ \mathrm I_Y/\mathrm I_Y^2\otimes_{\mathrm B}\mathrm C_0 \ar[r] \ar[uu] \ar[d]_-{a_{g_0}(c(X',Y',f))} & \mathrm I_{Y_0}/\mathrm I_{Y_0}^2\otimes_{B_0}\mathrm C_0 \ar[r] & J\mathrm C \ar[uu] \\ J\mathrm C & & } \] We thus have \(a_{g_0}(c(X',Y',f))(\overline x\otimes 1) = f(u)\), where \(u\) is an element of \(\mathrm I_{Y'}\) lifting \(g_0(x_0) = (g(x))_0 = y_0'\). We therefore take \(u = y'\) and we find \[ a_{g_0}(c(X',Y',f))(\overline x\otimes 1) = f(y'). \]

Consider finally \(b_{f_0}(c(X,Y,g\circ i'))\). It is defined by the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm I_Y/\mathrm I_Y^2\otimes_{\mathrm B}B_0' \ar[r]^-{\overline g} \ar[d] & J\mathrm B' \ar[d] \\ \mathrm I_Y/\mathrm I_Y^2\otimes_{\mathrm B}\mathrm C_0 \ar[r] \ar[dr]_-{b_{f_0}(c(X,Y,g\circ i'))} & J\mathrm B'\otimes_{B_0'}\mathrm C_0 \ar[d] & J\otimes_{\Lambda_0}\mathrm C_0 \ar[l]_-{\sim} \\ & J\mathrm C & } \] We thus obtain at once \[ b_{f_0}(c(X,Y,g\circ i'))(\overline x\otimes 1) = f(\overline g(x)) = \sum \lambda_i f(a_i'). \]

Comparison of the three explicit results gives the desired formula.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.14

Let Y and \(Y'\) be two flat subschemes of X reducing to \(Y_J\), and suppose \(Y_0\) locally a complete intersection in \(X_0\). If \(\mathrm T \xrightarrow{\ f\ } X\) is an S-morphism such that \(f_J\) factors through \(Y_J \to X_J\), we have the formula \[ c(X,Y',f) - c(X,Y,f) = f^*(d(Y',Y)) \] where \(f^*\) is the natural morphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0}, J\mathscr{O}_{Y}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(\mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0}, J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}). \]

Indeed, it suffices to apply the preceding formula to the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & Y' \ar@{^{(}->}[d]^-{i'} & & Y \ar@{^{(}->}[d]^-{i} \\ \mathrm T \ar[r]_-{f} & X \ar[rr]_-{\mathrm{id}} & & X } \] and to use the relation \(c(X,Y,i') = d(Y,Y')\) (cf. 4.8 (ii)).

blocks.title: Nota
blocks.html:
Nota

Taking \(f = i'\) in the preceding formula, one recovers the relation given in 4.8 (ii).

We shall now consider the case where X is an S-group smooth over S. Let us note first that every sub-S-group Y flat over S is locally a complete intersection in X. Let us sketch the proof of this fact. By virtue of general results (cf. in particular SGA 1 IV 5.7), as X and Y are flat, we are reduced to verifying the assertion on the geometric fibres of S, hence when S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field. One remarks next that we are dealing with groups, so that it suffices to verify the assertion at the origin. Finally, to verify that Y is, at the origin, a complete intersection in X, we may content ourselves with doing so for the formal groups \(\widehat X\) and \(\widehat Y\) corresponding to X and Y. As X is smooth, the hyperalgebra of \(\widehat X\) is a polynomial algebra, and one concludes with the help of the structure theorem of DIEUDONNÉ, which shows that the hyperalgebra of \(\widehat Y\) is obtained by "truncating" that of X, hence is a complete intersection in it (cf. Exp. VII). Of course, when Y is also smooth over S, it suffices simply to invoke SGA 1 II 4.15.

Let us now give ourselves a sub-\(S_J\)-group \(Y_J\) of \(X_J\), flat and locally of finite presentation over \(S_J\). We know first that \(Y_J\) is locally a complete intersection in X, hence (4.6) that every S-scheme Y lifting \(Y_J\) is locally a complete intersection in X, and that such a Y exists if in addition \(Y_0\) is affine.

Moreover, we have \(\mathscr{N}_{Y/X}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0} = \mathscr{N}_{Y_J/X_J}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_J}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0} = \mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0}\). As \(Y_0\) and \(X_0\) are \(S_0\)-groups, one sees easily that if we denote by \(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\) the inverse image of \(\mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0}\) by the unit section of \(Y_0\) (it is a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{S_0}\)-Module), we also have \[ \mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0} = \mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}. \]

The deviations and obstructions of 4.7 and 4.8 are thus elements, respectively, of the groups \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}}(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}, J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}) \] and \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{T_0}, J\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}). \] Consider the functor in commutative groups N over \(S_0\) defined by \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm Z,\mathrm N) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm Z}}(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm Z}, J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm Z}), \qquad \mathrm Z\in \mathrm{Ob}\,(\mathbf{Sch})_{/S_0}. \] We know that the set of sub-S-schemes of X, flat over S, lifting \(Y_J\), is empty or principal homogeneous under \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(Y_0,\mathrm N) = \mathrm C^1(Y_0,\mathrm N)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.17

Under the preceding hypotheses, consider for each Y lifting \(Y_J\) the obstruction \[ \mathrm{D}Y \in \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(Y_0\times_{S_0}Y_0, \mathrm N) = \mathrm C^2(Y_0,\mathrm N) \] defined by \(\mathrm D Y = c(X,Y,\pi\circ(i,i))\): \[ \xymatrix{ Y\underset{S}{\times}Y \ar[d]_-{(i,i)} & & Y \ar@{^{(}->}[d]^-{i} \\ X\underset{S}{\times}X \ar[rr]_-{\pi} & & X } \]

(i) For Y to be a sub-S-group of X, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathrm D Y = 0\).

(ii) If we let \(Y_0\) act on N by functoriality starting from the inner automorphisms of \(Y_0\), then \(\mathrm D Y \in \mathrm Z^2(Y_0,\mathrm N)\).

(iii) If Y and \(Y'\) are two subschemes of X, flat over S, lifting \(Y_J\) (so that the deviation \(d(Y,Y')\in \mathrm C^1(Y_0,\mathrm N)\) is defined), we have \(\mathrm D Y - \mathrm D Y' = \partial d(Y,Y')\).

Let us prove these various assertions in succession.

Proof of 4.17 (i). If \(\mathrm D Y = 0\), then there exists a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ Y\underset{S}{\times}Y \ar[r] \ar[d] & Y \ar[d] \\ X\underset{S}{\times}X \ar[r] & X } \] hence Y is stable under the group law of X. For it to be a subgroup of X, it is necessary and sufficient that it be a group for the induced law; now the latter is associative, and reduces modulo \(J\) to the law induced on \(X_J\), which is a group law. As Y is flat, one concludes by 3.3.

Proof of 4.17 (ii). This is done by comparing the two values of \(u = c(X,Y,\pi_3\circ(i,i,i))\) computed in the two following diagrams \(\mathrm D_j\), \(j=1,2\): \[ (\mathrm D_j)\qquad \xymatrix{ Y\times_S Y\times_S Y \ar[d]_-{(i,i,i)} & Y\times_S Y \ar[d]^-{(i,i)} & Y \ar[d]^-{i} \\ X\times_S X\times_S X \ar[r]_-{f_j} & X\times_S X \ar[r]_-{\pi} & X } \] where \(f_1=(1,\pi)\), \(f_2=(\pi,1)\), \(\pi\circ f_j = \pi_3 : X\times X\times X \to X\).

Each time one applies 4.13. We thus have \[ u = a_{1,\pi_0}((0,\mathrm D Y)) + b_{f_{1_0}}(\mathrm D Y) = a_{2,\pi_0}((\mathrm D Y,0)) + b_{f_{2_0}}(\mathrm D Y). \] The first thing one notices is that \(b_{f_{j_0}}\) is none other than \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(f_{j_0},\mathrm N)\), that is, the morphism deduced from \(f_{j_0}\) by functoriality. The identity above thus becomes, writing \(a_{j,\pi_0}=a_j\): \[ a_j : \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^3}}(f_{j_0}^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y_0\times Y_0/X_0\times X_0}), J\otimes\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^3}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^3}}(\pi_3^*(\mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0}), J\otimes\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^3}), \] \[ -a_1((0,\mathrm D Y)) + \mathrm{Hom}((\pi,1),\mathrm N)(\mathrm D Y) - \mathrm{Hom}((1,\pi),\mathrm N)(\mathrm D Y) + a_2((\mathrm D Y,0)) = 0. \] One recognizes the two middle terms: they are the parts "\(\mathrm D Y(xy,z)\)" and "\(\mathrm D Y(x,yz)\)" of the formula for the 2-coboundary. It thus only remains to identify the two other terms.

We must first compute the map \(a_j\). Now it comes, by inverse image under \(f_{j_0}\), from the morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^2}\)-modules \[ \mathrm P : \mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\otimes\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^2} \longrightarrow (\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\oplus\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0})\otimes\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^2} \] induced by the product in \(Y_0\). Now this morphism can be described in the following manner: consider the vector bundle \(\mathbf V(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}) = \mathrm V\). By duality, P gives a morphism \[ \mathbf V(\mathrm P) : \mathrm V\underset{S_0}{\times}\mathrm V\underset{S_0}{\times}Y_0\underset{S_0}{\times}Y_0 \longrightarrow \mathrm V\underset{S_0}{\times}Y_0\underset{S_0}{\times}Y_0 \] which is expressed set-theoretically by \[ \mathbf V(\mathrm P)(u,v,a,b) = (u+\mathrm{Ad}(a)v,\,a,\,b). \]

This is proved exactly like the corresponding fact for Lie Algebras, that is, for the Module \(\omega^1_{Y_0/S_0}\). One remarks first that V is endowed, by functoriality in \(Y_0\), with a group structure in the category of vector bundles over \(S_0\); by virtue of the lemma already used for Lie Algebras (Exposé II, 3.10), this structure coincides with the group structure underlying its \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module structure. One sees next that \(\mathbf V(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}) = \mathbf V(\mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0})\) is itself also endowed with an \(S_0\)-group structure, which is none other than the semi-direct product of that of V by that of \(Y_0\). It only remains to identify the actions of \(Y_0\) on V to establish the desired formula.

Let us now compute the two remaining terms. Consider first \(a_1((0,\mathrm D Y))\). It is computed by the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathfrak{n}\otimes\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^2} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm P} \ar[d]_-{f_{1_0}^*} & (\mathfrak{n}+\mathfrak{n})\otimes\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^2} \ar[d]^-{f_{1_0}^*} \\ \mathfrak{n}\otimes\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^3} \ar[dr]_-{a_1((0,\mathrm D Y))} & (\mathfrak{n}+\mathfrak{n})\otimes\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^3} \ar[d]^-{(0,\mathrm D Y)} \\ & J\otimes\mathscr{O}_{Y_0^3} } \]

Considering now the vector bundles defined by these various modules as so many schemes over \(S_0\), and taking points with values in anything whatsoever, we have, denoting by \((u,x,y,z)\) a point of \(\mathbf V(J)\times Y_0^3\): for the point \((u,x,y,z)\), the image under \(\mathbf V(\mathrm P)\) on the component \((0+\mathrm D Y_{y,z}(u),x,y,z)\) gives \((0+\mathrm D Y_{y,z}(u),x,yz)\) on the one hand, and by the adjoint action \((\mathrm{Ad}(x)\,\mathrm D Y_{y,z}(u),x,yz)\) on the other, these two expressions coinciding by construction of the morphism \(a_1\).

We have thus obtained \(a_1((0,\mathrm D Y))(x,y,z) = \mathrm{Ad}(x)\,\mathrm D Y(y,z)\), which is indeed the first term of the coboundary. One would similarly have \(a_2((\mathrm D Y,0))(x,y,z) = \mathrm D Y(x,y)\), whence \[ 0 = -\mathrm{Ad}(x)\,\mathrm D Y(y,z) + \mathrm D Y(xy,z) - \mathrm D Y(x,yz) + \mathrm D Y(x,y) = \partial\mathrm D Y(x,y,z). \]

Proof of 4.17 (iii). One considers the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ Y'\times Y' \ar[d] & Y\times Y \ar[d] & Y \ar[d] & Y' \ar[d] \\ X\times X \ar[r] & X\times X \ar[r] & X \ar[r] & X } \] which allows one to compute \(\mathrm D Y'\) from \(\mathrm D Y\) and \(d(Y,Y')\). The computation is done exactly as before; we leave the details to the reader. One finds \[ \mathrm D Y'(x,y) = d(Y',Y)(xy) + \mathrm D Y(x,y) - d(Y',Y)(x) - \mathrm{Ad}(x)\,d(Y',Y)(y) \] that is, \[ \mathrm D Y'(x,y) - \mathrm D Y(x,y) = -\mathrm{Ad}(x)\,d(Y',Y)(y) + d(Y',Y)(xy) - d(Y',Y)(x) = \partial d(Y',Y)(x,y). \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.21

Let S be a scheme, \(I\) and \(J\) two nilpotent ideals on S such that \(I\supset J\), \(I\cdot J=0\). Let X be an S-group smooth over S and \(Y_J\) a sub-\(S_J\)-group of \(X_J\), flat and locally of finite presentation over \(S_J\). Consider the \(S_0\)-functor in commutative groups \(\mathrm N_0\) defined by \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm N_0) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}}(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}, J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}), \qquad \mathrm T\in \mathrm{Ob}\,(\mathbf{Sch})_{/S_0}, \] on which \(Y_0\) acts through the inner automorphisms of \(X_0\).

(i) For there to exist a sub-S-group of X, flat over S, reducing to \(Y_J\), it is necessary and sufficient that the two following conditions be satisfied:

\((\mathrm i_1)\) There exists a subscheme Y of X, flat over S, lifting \(Y_J\) (a condition automatically satisfied if \(Y_0\) is affine, cf. 4.6).

\((\mathrm i_2)\) A certain canonical obstruction, an element of \(\mathrm H^2(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\), is zero.

(ii) If the conditions of (i) are satisfied, the set of sub-S-groups Y of X, flat over S and reducing to \(Y_J\), is a principal homogeneous set under the group \(\mathrm Z^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\).

Indeed, condition \((\mathrm i_1)\) is necessary. Suppose it satisfied and let Y be a flat lifting of \(Y_J\). We must look for a \(Y'\) also lifting \(Y_J\) such that \(\partial\mathrm D Y' = 0\) (4.17 (i)). But that amounts to looking for a \(d(Y,Y')\in \mathrm C^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\) such that \(\mathrm D Y = \partial d(Y,Y')\) (4.17 (iii)).

Let \(c\in \mathrm H^2(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\) be the class image of \(\mathrm D Y\), which is a cocycle by 4.17 (ii). It does not depend on the choice of Y, by 4.17 (iii), and its vanishing is necessary and sufficient for the existence of a \(d(Y,Y')\) satisfying the preceding equation. This proves (i). If we have now chosen Y such that \(\mathrm D Y = 0\), the equation to be solved reads \(\partial d(Y,Y') = 0\), which proves (ii).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.22

Note that \(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\) is of finite presentation over \(S_0\). Hence the functor \(\mathrm N_0\) can be written, on the category of S-flat schemes, \[ \mathrm N_0 = \mathbf W(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}, J)). \] From this result isomorphisms: \[ \mathrm H^2(Y_0,\mathrm N_0) \simeq \mathrm H^2(Y_0, \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}, J)), \] \[ \mathrm Z^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0) \simeq \mathrm Z^1(Y_0, \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}, J)). \]

Still under the hypotheses of 4.21, we shall now study how the set of Y lifting \(Y_J\) behaves with respect to conjugation by sections of X. If \(x\) is a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\), the inner automorphism \(\mathrm{Int}(x)\) defined by \(x\) transforms flat subgroups of X lifting \(Y_J\) into flat subgroups of X lifting \(Y_J\). Now, under the conditions of 4.21 (ii), the set of these subgroups is principal homogeneous under \(\mathrm Z^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\); we shall see that there then exists a subgroup of \(\mathrm Z^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\) such that two subgroups of X, flat over S and lifting \(Y_J\), are conjugate (by those \(x\in X(S)\) inducing the unit of \(X(S_J)\)) if and only if their "difference" in \(\mathrm Z^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\) is an element of this subgroup. In the best cases, we shall show that the latter is none other than \(\mathrm B^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\), hence that the set of subgroups of X, flat over S, lifting \(Y_J\), modulo conjugation by the \(x\in X(S)\) inducing the unit of \(X(S_J)\), is empty or principal homogeneous under \(\mathrm H^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\) (Proposition 4.36).

Let Y be a flat subgroup of X, reducing to \(Y_J\). Recall that we introduced in 0.5 the functor \(\mathrm L_{0X}\) (resp. \(\mathrm L_{0Y}\)) defined by the following identity, with respect to the variable \(S_0\)-scheme T: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm L_{0X}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}, J\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm T}) \] (resp. the same with X replaced by Y), as well as the functor \(\mathrm L'_X = \prod_{S_0/S}\mathrm L_{0X}\).
Now we have:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.25

There exists a canonical exact sequence of \(Y_0\)-modules \[ (+) \qquad \mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0} \xrightarrow{\ d\ } \omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\longrightarrow \omega^1_{Y_0/S_0}\longrightarrow 0, \] having the following properties:

(i) By inverse image on Y, \(d\) gives the morphism D of 4.8 (iii).

(ii) If \(X_0\) and \(Y_0\) are smooth over \(S_0\), then \(d\) is injective. As the two \(\omega^1\) are then locally free of finite type, the same holds for \(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}\), and the sequence is locally split.

By SGA 1 II, formula (4.3), there exists a canonical exact sequence of \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\)-modules \[ \mathscr{N}_{Y_0/X_0} \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm D\ } \Omega^1_{X_0/S_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{X_0}}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0} \longrightarrow \Omega^1_{Y_0/S_0}\longrightarrow 0. \] As this sequence is invariant under the translations of \(Y_0\), it comes from a sequence (+) over \(S_0\) by inverse image under the projection of \(Y_0\) onto \(S_0\). Letting now the inner automorphisms of \(Y_0\) act, one sees that (+) is an exact sequence of \(Y_0\)-modules. As (+) becomes exact after tensoring with \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_0}\), it is already exact (\(Y_0\) being faithfully flat over \(S_0\)). For the same reason, \(d\) is injective if and only if D is. The rest follows from SGA 1 II 4.10.

For every \(S_0\)-scheme T, (+) gives by functoriality an exact sequence of abelian groups \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm L_{0Y}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm L_{0X}) \xrightarrow{\ d_{\mathrm T}\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm N_0). \] In particular, taking for T the cartesian powers of \(Y_0\), we deduce an exact sequence of complexes of abelian groups: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm C^*(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0Y}) \longrightarrow \mathrm C^*(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X}) \xrightarrow{\ d^*\ } \mathrm C^*(Y_0,\mathrm N_0), \] and in particular a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0Y}) \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\partial} & \mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X}) \ar[r]^-{d^0} \ar[d]_-{\partial} & \mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm N_0) \ar[d]^-{\partial} \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mathrm C^1(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0Y}) \ar[r] & \mathrm C^1(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X}) \ar[r]_-{d^1} & \mathrm C^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0) } \]

Let us remark that \(\mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0Y})\) (resp. \(\mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X})\)) is none other than \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(S_0,\mathrm L_{0Y})\) (resp. \(\cdots\)), that is, the group of sections of Y (resp. X) over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\). Note also that \(d^1\) is none other than the morphism \(v_{i_{Y_0}}\) of 4.8 (iii), where \(i_{Y_0} : Y_0 \to X_0\) is the canonical immersion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.27

Under the conditions of 4.21 for S, \(I\), \(J\) and X, let Y be a subgroup of X, flat over S and lifting \(Y_J\). Let \(x \in \mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X})\) be a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\). Then \(d(Y,\mathrm{Int}(x)Y) \in \mathrm C^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\) is given by the formula \[ d(\mathrm{Int}(x)Y,Y) = \partial\,d^0 x = d^1\partial x. \]

Indeed, set \(Y' = \mathrm{Int}(x)Y\). By the formula \[ xyx^{-1} = xyx^{-1}y^{-1}y = (x-\mathrm{Ad}(y)x)y = -(\partial x)(y)\cdot y, \] one sees that \(Y'\) can be described as the image of Y under the composite morphism (cf. 4.8 (iii)): \[ Y \xrightarrow{\ (-\partial x,\,i_Y)\ } \mathrm L'_X\underset{S}{\times}X \longrightarrow X. \]

If \(i_Y\) denotes the canonical immersion of Y into X, we have, by 4.8 (iii) and the preceding result: \[ c(X,Y',\mathrm{Int}(x)\circ i_Y) - c(X,Y',i_Y) = -d^1\partial x. \] But \(\mathrm{Int}(x)\circ i_Y\) factors through \(Y'\) by definition and the first term is zero; by 4.8 (ii), we have \(c(X,Y',i_Y) = d(Y',Y)\). This implies \(d(Y',Y) = d^1\partial x\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.28

For two subgroups Y and \(Y'\) of G, flat over S and lifting \(Y_J\), to be conjugate by a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(d(Y,Y')\in \partial\,d^0\,\mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X})\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.29

If \(d^0\) is surjective, Y and \(Y'\) as above are conjugate by a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\) if and only if \(d(Y,Y')\in \mathrm B^1(Y_0,\mathrm N_0)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.30

Let Y be as in 4.27; the set of conjugates of Y by sections of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\) is isomorphic to: \[ d^1\partial\,\mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X}) = \mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X})\big/\mathrm{Ker}\,d^1\partial. \]

Let us now remark that \(\mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X})/\mathrm{Ker}\,d^1\partial\) is computed solely by means of the left-hand square of the commutative diagram of 4.26. It follows in particular that one may also compute it in any diagram of the same type having the same left-hand square. Consider in particular the functor \(\mathrm L_{0X}/\mathrm L_{0Y}\) over \(S_0\) defined by \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm L_{0X}/\mathrm L_{0Y}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm L_{0X})/\mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm L_{0Y}). \] We have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathrm C^0(\mathrm L_{0Y}) \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\partial} & \mathrm C^0(\mathrm L_{0X}) \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{\partial} & \mathrm C^0(\mathrm L_{0X}/\mathrm L_{0Y}) \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{\partial} & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mathrm C^1(\mathrm L_{0Y}) \ar[r] & \mathrm C^1(\mathrm L_{0X}) \ar[r] & \mathrm C^1(\mathrm L_{0X}/\mathrm L_{0Y}) \ar[r] & 0 } \] whence, by the preceding remark:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.31

Let Y be as in 4.27; the set of conjugates of Y by sections of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\) is isomorphic to \[ \mathrm E = \partial\,\mathrm C^0(\mathrm L_{0X}/\mathrm L_{0Y}) = \mathrm C^0(\mathrm L_{0X}/\mathrm L_{0Y})\big/\mathrm H^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X}/\mathrm L_{0Y}). \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.32

Suppose moreover \(S_0\) affine and \(\mathscr{L}ie(Y_0/S_0)\) locally free. If we set \(\mathscr{F}_0 = [\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)/\mathscr{L}ie(Y_0/S_0)]\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}J\), we have \(\mathrm E = \Gamma(S_0,\mathscr{F}_0)/\mathrm H^0(Y_0,\mathscr{F}_0)\).

Indeed, for every T over \(S_0\) we have an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm L_{0Y}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(\mathrm T,\mathrm L_{0X}) \longrightarrow \mathbf W(\mathscr{F}_0)(\mathrm T). \] By the reasoning that served us to prove 4.31, we may compute E as the image of \(\mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X})\) in \(\mathrm C^1(Y_0,\mathbf W(\mathscr{F}))\). But as \(S_0\) is affine, \(\mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X}) \to \mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathbf W(\mathscr{F}))\) is surjective, and we indeed find the announced result.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.33

Let S, \(I\), \(J\) and X be as in 4.21. Suppose S affine. Let Y be a diagonalizable subgroup of X. The set of subgroups of X conjugate to Y by a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\) is isomorphic to \[ \mathrm E = \Gamma\Bigl(S_0, \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)/\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)}\Bigr)\otimes_{\Gamma(S_0,\mathscr{O}_{S_0})} \Gamma(S_0,J). \]

Indeed, one writes, by I 4.7.3 (cf. 2.13), \[ \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0) = \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)} \oplus \mathscr{R}. \] As \(Y_0\) is commutative, we have \(\mathscr{L}ie(Y_0/S_0) \subset \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)}\), hence \[ \mathscr{F}_0 = \Bigl(\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)}\big/\mathscr{L}ie(Y_0/S_0)\Bigr)\otimes J \oplus \mathscr{R}\otimes J, \] \[ \mathscr{F}_0^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)} = \Bigl(\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)^{\mathrm{ad}(Y_0)}\big/\mathscr{L}ie(Y_0/S_0)\Bigr)\otimes J. \]

By 4.32, we thus have \(\mathrm E \simeq \Gamma(S_0,\mathscr{R}\otimes J)\). Going back to the definition of \(\mathscr{R}\), we are done.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.34

Let S, \(I\), \(J\) and X be as in 4.21. Suppose S affine. Let Y be a diagonalizable subgroup of X. If \(x\in X(S)\) induces the unit section of \(X_J\) and normalizes Y, then it centralizes Y.

This follows immediately from a comparison of the preceding corollary with 2.14. Indeed, 4.33 shows that the elements of \(\mathrm C^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X})\) which preserve Y globally are the elements of \(\mathrm H^0(Y_0,\mathrm L_{0X})\), and we saw in 2.14 that these are exactly the ones that act trivially on the canonical immersion \(Y \to X\).

Let us return to the general situation of 4.21 and suppose \(Y_J\) smooth over \(S_J\). Then (SGA 1 II 4.10), every subscheme Y of X lifting \(Y_J\) and flat will be smooth over S. Moreover, by 4.25 (ii), we have an isomorphism of \(Y_0\)-modules: \[ \mathfrak{n}^{\vee}_{Y_0/X_0} = \mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)/\mathscr{L}ie(Y_0/S_0). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.36

Under the hypotheses of 4.21, suppose moreover \(Y_J\) smooth over \(S_J\) and \(S_0\) affine. The set of sub-S-groups Y of X flat (or smooth) over S, reducing to \(Y_J\), modulo conjugation by sections of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_J\), is either empty, or a principal homogeneous set under the group \[ \mathrm H^1\bigl(Y_0,[\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)/\mathscr{L}ie(Y_0/S_0)]\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}J\bigr). \]

It suffices for us to verify that Corollary 4.29 applies, that is, that \[ d^0 : \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\omega^1_{X_0/S_0}, J) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}(\mathfrak{n}_{Y_0/X_0}, J) \] is surjective. Now this follows from the fact that the sequence (+) of 4.25 (ii) is split, \(S_0\) being affine.
Let us finally state a corollary common to 4.21 and 4.36, which will be, in fact, the only form in which we shall use the general results of this section in the sequel.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.37

Let S be a scheme and \(S_0\) the closed subscheme defined by a nilpotent ideal \(I\). Let X be an S-group smooth over S, and \(Y_0\) a sub-\(S_0\)-group of \(X_0\), flat over \(S_0\).

(i) If \(S_0\) is affine, \(Y_0\) smooth over \(S_0\), and if \[ \mathrm H^1\bigl(Y_0,[\mathscr{L}ie(X_0/S_0)/\mathscr{L}ie(Y_0/S_0)]\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}I^{n+1}/I^{n+2}\bigr) = 0 \] for every \(n\geqslant 0\), two sub-S-groups of X, flat (or smooth) over S, reducing to \(Y_0\), are conjugate by a section of X over S inducing the unit section of \(X_0\).

(ii) If \(Y_0\) is affine and of finite presentation and if \[ \mathrm H^2\bigl(Y_0,\mathfrak{n}^{\vee}_{Y_0/X_0}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}}I^{n+1}/I^{n+2}\bigr) = 0 \] for every \(n\geqslant 0\), there exists a sub-S-group of X, flat over S, reducing to \(Y_0\).

It remains for us to relate the three constructions we have made in this exposé. To avoid inessential complications, we shall place ourselves in the following situation: \(S_0\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), S is the spectrum of the dual numbers \(\mathrm D(k)\), X is an S-group smooth over S, Y a sub-S-group, affine and smooth over S. We have an exact sequence of \(k\)-vector spaces: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0 \xrightarrow{\ i\ } \mathscr{L}ie\,X_0 \xrightarrow{\ d\ } \mathfrak{n}^{\vee}_{Y_0/X_0} \longrightarrow 0, \] giving rise to an exact cohomology sequence (where we write \(\mathrm H^i(\ )\) for \(\mathrm H^i(Y_0,\ )\)): \[ 0 \to \mathrm H^0(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0) \xrightarrow{i^0} \mathrm H^0(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0) \xrightarrow{d^0} \mathrm H^0(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0/\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0) \] \[ \xrightarrow{\partial^0} \mathrm H^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0) \xrightarrow{i^1} \mathrm H^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0) \xrightarrow{d^1} \mathrm H^1(Y_0,\mathfrak{n}^{\vee}_{Y_0/X_0}) \xrightarrow{\partial^1} \mathrm H^2(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0). \]
Now these various groups all have a geometric meaning.
a) \(\mathrm H^0(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0) = \mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(Y)_0\) by (Exp. II 5.2.3).
b) \(\mathrm H^0(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0) = \mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_X(Y)_0\), (id.).
c) \(\mathrm H^0(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0/\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0) = \mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_X(Y)_0/\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0\), (id.).

d) \(\mathrm H^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0) = \mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(Y)_0/\mathrm{Im}(\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0)\), where \(\mathrm{Im}(\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0)\) denotes the image of \(\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0\) under \(\mathscr{L}ie(\mathrm{Int})_0\) deduced from \[ \mathrm{Int} : Y_0 \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(Y)\, ; \] this follows at once from 2.1 and II 4.2.

e) \(\mathrm H^1(\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0)\) = the group of deviations between homomorphisms \(Y \to X\) extending the canonical immersion \(i_0 : Y_0 \to X_0\), modulo the deviations obtained by the action of the inner automorphisms of X defined by elements of X(S) giving the unit of \(X(S_0)\) (that is, elements of \(\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0\)). (cf. 2.1 and 1.2.2).

f) \(\mathrm H^1(\mathfrak{n}^{\vee}_{Y_0/X_0})\) = the group of deviations between subgroups \(Y'\) of X, flat over S, extending \(Y_0\), modulo the deviations obtained by the action of the inner automorphisms of X constructed as before. (cf. proof of 4.21).
g) \(\mathrm H^2(\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0)\) = the group of deviations between group structures on Y extending that of \(Y_0\), modulo the S-automorphisms of Y inducing the identity on \(Y_0\) (cf. 3.5).
We now propose to show how one can make explicit the 6 morphisms of the preceding exact sequence in the geometric interpretation we have just given.

1) \(i^0\) and \(d^0\) are none other than the morphisms obtained by passage to the Lie Algebra (then by passage to the quotient for \(d^0\)), from the canonical monomorphisms: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(Y) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_X(Y) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_X(Y). \] This is indeed what follows immediately from the definition of the identifications (a), (b), and (c).

2) One constructs \(\partial^0\) as follows. Let \(\overline x \in \mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_X(Y)_0/\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0\). Let us lift it to an \(x\in \mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_X(Y)_0 \subset \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_X(Y)\). Then \(\mathrm{Int}(x)\) defines an automorphism of Y inducing the identity on \(Y_0\), hence an element of \(\mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(Y)_0\). Denote by \(\overline{\mathrm{Int}(x)}\) the image of this element in \(\mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(Y)_0/\mathrm{Im}(\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0)\). Then we have: \[ \partial^0(x) = -\overline{\mathrm{Int}(x)} = \overline{\mathrm{Int}(x^{-1})}. \]

Indeed, let us compute the element of \(\mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(Y)_0\) defined by \(\mathrm{Int}(x)\). It will correspond, by definition, to an element \(a\) of \(\mathrm Z^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0)\) such that \[ xyx^{-1} = a(y_0)y, \qquad y\in Y(S'),\ S'\longrightarrow S. \] But this can also be written \(a(y_0) = xyx^{-1}y^{-1} = x - \mathrm{ad}(y)x = -\partial x(y_0)\).

3) Let \(\overline u\) be an element of \(\mathrm H^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0)\), the canonical image of a \[ u \in \mathscr{L}ie\,\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(Y)_0 \subset \mathrm{Aut}_{S\text{-gr.}}(Y). \] Then, if \(i : Y \to X\) is the canonical immersion, we have: \[ i^1(\overline u) = d(i,u\circ i). \]

Indeed, \(d(i,u\circ i)\) is defined as the image of an element \(d\in \mathrm Z^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0)\) such that \[ iu(y) = d(y_0)i(y). \] Now \(\overline u\) is defined as the image of the element \(v\in \mathrm Z^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0)\) such that \[ u(y) = v(y_0)y. \] We may therefore choose \(d(y_0) = iv(y_0)\), which proves the stated relation.

4) Let \(\overline d(i,i')\) be the image of the deviation \(d(i,i')\) in \(\mathrm H^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0)\), where \(i' : Y \to X\) is a group homomorphism lifting \(i_0\). Let \(\overline d(Y,i'(Y))\) be the image of the deviation \(d(Y,i'(Y))\) in \(\mathrm H^1(Y_0,\mathfrak{n}^{\vee}_{Y_0/X_0})\). Then \[ d^1(\overline d(i,i')) = \overline d(Y,i'(Y)). \] This follows without difficulty from 4.8 (ii) and (iii), reasoning as in the proof of 4.27.

5) Finally, let \(Y'\) be a subgroup of X, flat over S and lifting \(Y_0\). We have supposed that \(Y_0\) is affine. Then we know that Y and \(Y'\) are isomorphic as S-schemes extending \(Y_0\) (3.6), hence that there exists an isomorphism of S-schemes \[ f : Y \longrightarrow Y' \] inducing the identity on \(Y_0\). Let us transport the group structure of \(Y'\) by \(f\), and let \(Y_1\) be the group obtained (which therefore has Y as underlying scheme). Then \[ \partial^1\overline d(Y,Y') = \overline d(Y,Y_1). \]

Indeed, \(d(Y,Y_1)\) is defined as the image of a \(b\in \mathrm Z^2(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0)\) such that \[ f^{-1}(f(y)f(y')) = b(y_0,y_0')\,yy'. \] On the other hand, \(d(Y,Y')\) is defined as the image in \(\mathrm H^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0/\mathscr{L}ie\,Y_0)\) of an \(a\in \mathrm Z^1(Y_0,\mathscr{L}ie\,X_0)\) such that \(f(y) = a(y_0)y\). Computing \(b\), one finds at once: \[ b(y_0,y_0') = a(y_0y_0')^{-1}\,a(y_0)\,y\,a(y_0')\,y'\,(yy')^{-1} = \partial a(y_0,y_0'). \]
